description: Cooling or temperature alarms caused by room heat, blocked airflow, chiller issues, water flow problems, or system thermal limits.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: If the CT scanner is alarming during patient setup or scanning, stop use until the cause is understood. Action: Move the patient off the table if scanning cannot continue safely. Cancel or delay the exam if tube cooling, gantry cooling, or system temperature alarms are active. Notify CT staff that the scanner should not be used until cleared. Expected outcome: The patient is safely removed from a scanner that may be unable to complete imaging reliably. Why it matters: Cooling and temperature faults can lead to scan interruption, exposure inhibit, tube protection shutdowns, image quality issues, or equipment damage. If this resolves an immediate patient safety concern, stop clinical use and continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Alarm or Temperature Message
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm, warning, or system message. Action: Ask CT staff what message appeared. Check whether the alarm occurred during startup, idle, tube warm-up, scanning, or after repeated high-load exams. Record any displayed temperature, cooling, chiller, gantry, or tube heat message. Note whether scanning is inhibited or only delayed. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified as a cooling, temperature, or thermal protection problem. Why it matters: A tube heat warning, room temperature warning, chiller fault, and internal gantry temperature alarm may all require different responses.

steps.title: 3. Check the CT Room Temperature
steps.instructions: Verify that the scan room is within the site’s required environmental range. Action: Check the room thermostat or independent temperature reading. Ask staff if the room recently became unusually warm. Check whether the HVAC system is operating normally. Look for blocked vents, closed dampers, or temporary airflow restrictions. Expected outcome: The room temperature is stable and appropriate for CT scanner operation. Why it matters: CT scanners generate significant heat. A warm room can cause the system to reach thermal limits even when the scanner itself is functioning normally. If the room is too warm, contact Facilities/HVAC, keep the scanner out of clinical use if alarms persist, and recheck after the room stabilizes.

steps.title: 4. Inspect Airflow Around the Gantry and Equipment Cabinets
steps.instructions: Check for blocked vents or poor ventilation around the scanner. Action: Inspect the gantry, console area, power cabinets, and equipment cabinets. Remove any linens, boxes, cleaning supplies, paperwork, or stored items near vents. Confirm that required clearance around cabinets and vents is not obstructed. Listen for abnormal fan noise if accessible externally. Expected outcome: Air intake and exhaust paths are clear, and airflow is not blocked. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can cause internal temperature alarms, cabinet overheating, or intermittent shutdowns. If clearing the obstruction resolves the alarm and the system passes normal startup checks, return to service per site procedure.

steps.title: 5. Check for Obvious Chiller or Cooling Unit Problems
steps.instructions: If the site uses an external chiller or cooling unit, inspect it externally only. Action: Verify the chiller is powered on. Check for visible alarms or fault indicators on the chiller display. Confirm the emergency stop or disconnect has not been activated. Look for visible leaks, water on the floor, kinked hoses, or disconnected lines. Confirm the chiller area has adequate airflow and is not blocked by storage. Expected outcome: The chiller is powered, not visibly leaking, and not showing an obvious external fault. Why it matters: Loss of chilled water flow, chiller power, or coolant circulation can trigger scanner temperature alarms and prevent scanning. If the chiller has active faults, leaks, or is not running, remove the scanner from service and escalate to Facilities, OEM service, or the appropriate cooling system vendor.

steps.title: 6. Check Cooling Water or Coolant Indicators
steps.instructions: Review any externally visible coolant level, pressure, or flow indicators if available at your site. Action: Look for low-level indicators, pressure gauges, or flow alarms without opening service panels. Do not add coolant unless site procedure and manufacturer guidance allow it. Check whether staff recently reported water leaks, pump noise, or repeated cooling faults. Expected outcome: No obvious low coolant, flow, or pressure issue is seen externally. Why it matters: Low coolant, poor circulation, or reduced flow may allow the scanner to overheat even if the room temperature is normal. If coolant level, pressure, or flow appears abnormal, stop troubleshooting and escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Review Scanner Use Pattern Before the Alarm
steps.instructions: Determine whether the scanner was under heavy thermal load. Action: Ask whether the alarm occurred after multiple contrast studies, trauma scans, cardiac studies, long helical scans, or repeated rescans. Check whether the system displayed tube heat or cooling delay messages. Allow the scanner to cool if the system instructs a cooling wait period. Expected outcome: The scanner either cools down normally after a high-load period or the alarm persists beyond normal cooldown. Why it matters: Some temperature warnings are protective delays after heavy tube usage, while persistent alarms may indicate a cooling system fault. If the scanner cools normally and no alarm returns, document the event and return to service per site policy.

steps.title: 8. Perform a Controlled System Restart Only If Safe
steps.instructions: A software or sensor communication fault can sometimes present as a temperature warning. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow normal shutdown procedures from the operator console. Allow the system to power down completely. Restart the system and observe for repeated cooling or temperature alarms. Expected outcome: The alarm clears if it was a temporary communication or software state issue. Why it matters: A restart can clear transient faults, but it should not be used to bypass a real overheating or coolant problem. If the alarm returns, do not continue restarting repeatedly.

steps.title: 9. Check for Odor, Heat, Smoke, or Unusual Noise
steps.instructions: Look for signs of equipment damage or unsafe operation. Action: Stand near the gantry, cabinets, and chiller area without opening covers. Check for unusual heat, burning smell, coolant odor, smoke, loud fan noise, pump noise, or vibration. Ask staff whether the alarm was accompanied by shutdowns or abnormal sounds. Expected outcome: No unsafe smell, heat, smoke, leak, or abnormal noise is present. Why it matters: These signs may indicate fan failure, pump failure, electrical overheating, coolant leak, or internal component damage. If smoke, burning smell, fluid leak, or abnormal heat is present, remove the scanner from service immediately and escalate.

steps.title: 10. Confirm Scanner Status After Basic Corrections
steps.instructions: After room temperature, airflow, power, and chiller checks are complete, confirm whether the scanner can operate normally. Action: Verify the alarm is cleared. Confirm the system completes startup without temperature warnings. Ask CT staff to perform approved daily checks, warm-up, or air calibration only if safe and allowed by site procedure. Monitor for alarm recurrence during idle and normal preparation. Expected outcome: The scanner remains stable with no cooling or temperature alarms. Why it matters: A one-time reset is not enough. The scanner must remain thermally stable before being returned to clinical use. If the alarm does not return and all checks pass, document the corrective action and return the scanner to service.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported a cooling system temperature alarm on the Philips Ingenuity CT scanner during exam setup, preventing scanning.
CCR.Cause: Room temperature was elevated and airflow around the equipment cabinet was partially blocked by stored supplies.
CCR.Resolution: Removed obstruction, notified Facilities to correct room temperature, allowed system to stabilize, restarted scanner, and verified no temperature alarm returned.

helpfulDetails: Air vents and cabinet clearances checked
Chiller powered on or showing fault
When the alarm occurred during workflow
Unusual heat, odor, vibration, or noise

title: Philips Patient Information Center iX Central Monitoring Station - Bed Not Connected / No Data From Bedside Monitor
description: Troubleshooting missing bedside monitor data, disconnected beds, or no patient information displaying at the central station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being monitored at the bedside before troubleshooting the central station. Action: Verify that the bedside monitor is powered on, displaying patient data, and actively alarming locally if needed. If the patient requires continuous monitoring and the central station is not receiving data, notify clinical staff immediately. Have staff monitor the patient directly at the bedside or move the patient to another monitored bed if needed. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained even if central station visibility is lost. Why it matters: A central station connection issue can delay alarm awareness if staff assume the patient is being monitored centrally. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Bed and Symptom
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly which bed is missing and what the central station is showing. Action: Check whether the issue affects: One bed only. Multiple beds. One sector/view. The entire central station. Look for messages such as: Bed not connected. No data. Offline. Blank waveform area. Missing patient name or vitals. Expected outcome: You determine whether this is likely a single bedside monitor issue, network issue, central station display issue, or configuration issue. Why it matters: One missing bed points toward bedside monitor, network drop, bed assignment, or cabling. Multiple missing beds may point toward network, server, switch, or central station communication issues.

steps.title: Confirm the Bedside Monitor Is Powered On and Functioning
steps.instructions: Check the bedside monitor before assuming the central station has failed. Action: Go to the bedside monitor. Confirm the monitor is powered on and not frozen. Verify that waveforms, numerics, alarms, and patient information are visible locally. Check for local network, communication, or system error messages. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor is functioning normally at the patient location. If the bedside monitor is off, frozen, rebooting, or not monitoring correctly, address that issue first and stop central station troubleshooting until bedside operation is restored.

steps.title: Check the Bedside Monitor Network Connection
steps.instructions: Verify the physical communication path from the bedside monitor. Action: Inspect the network cable at the bedside monitor or docking station. Confirm the cable is fully seated and not damaged. Check the wall data jack connection. Look for link/activity lights if visible on the network port, docking station, or network adapter. If safe and approved, reseat the network cable. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor has a solid physical network connection. If reseating the cable restores central station data, document the loose connection and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Bed Is Assigned Correctly at the Central Station
steps.instructions: Check whether the bed is assigned, hidden, discharged, or displayed in the wrong location. Action: Review the central station sector, unit layout, or bed overview. Confirm the correct bed label is selected. Check whether the patient or bed was recently transferred, discharged, admitted, or moved. Ask staff if the bedside monitor was swapped, moved, or reassigned. Expected outcome: The correct bedside monitor is mapped to the correct central station bed location. If the bed assignment was incorrect and correcting it restores data, document the assignment issue and stop.

steps.title: Check for a Local Bedside Monitor Swap or Room Move
steps.instructions: Determine whether equipment was recently changed. Action: Ask staff whether: The bedside monitor was replaced. The patient was moved rooms. The monitor was unplugged for transport. A transport monitor was returned to the room. The bed label changed after cleaning or patient transfer. Expected outcome: You identify whether the central station is looking for the old monitor, old bed, or wrong device. If the issue started after a move or swap, verify bed association and network connection before escalating.

steps.title: Compare With Neighboring Beds
steps.instructions: Use nearby working beds to narrow the issue. Action: Check whether other beds on the same unit are visible at the central station. Check whether beds connected to the same network area or switch appear normal. Confirm whether only one room, one group of rooms, or the whole unit is affected. Expected outcome: You determine whether the fault is isolated or widespread. Why it matters: A single failed bed usually points to bedside monitor, cable, jack, or assignment. A group of failed beds may indicate network switch, VLAN, server, or infrastructure trouble.

steps.title: Check the Central Station Display View
steps.instructions: Make sure the bed is not simply filtered, hidden, or placed on another screen. Action: Review the active screen layout. Check other sectors, tabs, or unit views. Confirm the bed has not been removed from the displayed layout. If there are multiple central stations, compare whether the bed appears on another Philips Patient Information Center iX workstation. Expected outcome: The bed is either confirmed missing from all expected central views or found in a different view. If the bed appears elsewhere, correct the view or notify the appropriate clinical lead.

steps.title: Verify Central Station Network Status
steps.instructions: Check whether the central station itself appears connected and receiving other data. Action: Confirm the Patient Information Center iX workstation is powered on and responsive. Check whether other monitored beds are updating normally. Look for system messages, network warnings, server communication errors, or frozen displays. Confirm the workstation is connected to the network and has not been unplugged or moved. Expected outcome: The central station is functioning and receiving data from other beds. If the entire central station is not receiving data, remove reliance on that station and escalate to Clinical Engineering, IT/network support, or Philips service as appropriate.

steps.title: Reboot Only When Clinically Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: Do not reboot monitoring equipment during active patient care unless the clinical impact is understood. Action: If the bedside monitor is not monitoring correctly and staff agree it is safe, reboot the bedside monitor according to facility procedure. If the central station workstation is frozen or not updating any beds, coordinate with clinical leadership before rebooting. Ensure patients remain monitored locally or through an alternate central station during any restart. Expected outcome: Communication may restore after a safe restart if the issue was caused by a temporary software or communication lockup. If rebooting restores the connection, verify stable data flow before returning the issue to service.

steps.title: Escalate Network or System-Level Failures
steps.instructions: If multiple beds are affected or the same issue repeats, treat it as a system communication problem. Action: Escalate to the appropriate support path for: Network switch or port failure. Server communication issue. Incorrect bed configuration. Clinical network outage. Philips monitoring system service issue. Document which beds are affected, when the issue started, and whether bedside monitors still show local data. Expected outcome: The issue is escalated with enough detail for IT, networking, Clinical Engineering, or vendor support to troubleshoot efficiently.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that Bed 12 was not connected at the Philips Patient Information Center iX and no bedside monitor data was displaying at the central station.
CCR.Cause: Bedside monitor was powered on and monitoring locally, but the network cable at the bedside wall jack was loose and had no link activity.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the bedside network connection, verified link activity restored, confirmed Bed 12 waveforms and numerics returned to the Patient Information Center iX, and returned the monitoring pathway to service.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or power status of bedside monitor
Whether bedside monitor displayed data locally
Whether one bed or multiple beds were affected
Whether the patient was moved, discharged, admitted, or transferred
Whether the bedside monitor was swapped
Whether other central stations showed the same bed
Any frozen screens or delayed updates

title: Philips Patient Information Center iX Central Monitoring Station - Patient Sector Blank or Missing
description: Troubleshooting a blank or missing patient sector caused by assignment, network, display, bed label, or central station communication issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on a central station sector that is blank, missing, or not displaying the correct patient. Action: Confirm the patient is still being monitored at the bedside monitor. Ask clinical staff to verify alarms and waveforms directly at the bedside. If central monitoring is required and unavailable, follow the department’s patient monitoring escalation process. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the central station display issue is investigated. Why it matters: A missing patient sector can create a false sense that the patient is not connected, discharged, or not being monitored.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is missing. Action: Check whether: The entire patient sector is blank. The sector is present but has no waveforms or numerics. The bed label is missing. The wrong bed is showing. Only one patient sector is affected. Multiple sectors are affected. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a single bed assignment, one sector layout, or a broader central/network issue. If the sector reappears after confirming the display view or layout, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Bedside Monitor Is Powered On and Monitoring
steps.instructions: Check the bedside monitor first before assuming the central station is the problem. Action: Go to the patient room or ask clinical staff to verify that the bedside monitor is powered on. Confirm the bedside monitor has active ECG, SpO2, NIBP, or other expected parameters. Check for local alarms or communication messages at the bedside. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor is active and capable of sending patient data. If the bedside monitor is off, disconnected, or not monitoring the patient, restore bedside monitoring first, then recheck the central station.

steps.title: Check Network or Communication Status at the Bedside Monitor
steps.instructions: A central station sector may go blank if the bedside monitor is not communicating on the monitoring network. Action: Look for network, central monitoring, or communication indicators on the bedside monitor. Confirm the network cable is connected if the monitor uses a wired connection. If wireless monitoring is used, confirm the device is connected to the correct clinical network and has adequate signal. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor shows normal communication with the monitoring network. If a loose cable or wireless communication issue is corrected and the sector returns, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Patient or Bed Assignment
steps.instructions: A missing sector may be caused by an incorrect bed assignment, discharged patient, or patient assigned to a different central station view. Action: At the central station, verify the correct unit, care area, patient list, and bed label. Check whether the patient was admitted, discharged, transferred, or moved to another bed. Ask clinical staff whether the patient was recently moved or reassigned. Expected outcome: The central station is looking for the correct patient and correct bed. If the patient was assigned to the wrong bed or discharged in the system, have the appropriate clinical workflow corrected and recheck the sector.

steps.title: Check Central Station View, Layout, and Sector Configuration
steps.instructions: The patient may not be missing from the system; the display layout may simply not be showing that bed. Action: Check whether the central station is on the correct screen, unit view, or patient sector layout. Look for available beds that may be hidden, minimized, off-screen, or on another display page. Confirm the expected number of sectors are visible. Expected outcome: The correct care area and sector layout are displayed. If changing back to the correct view restores the patient sector, document the layout/view issue and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Display Monitor Is Working Properly
steps.instructions: A sector may appear missing if part of the display is not visible or if a secondary monitor is off. Action: Check that all central station display monitors are powered on. Confirm video cables are secure. Check for a frozen screen, black display, wrong input source, or monitor sleep mode. If multiple displays are used, verify the missing sector is not located on another screen. Expected outcome: The central station displays all expected sectors across the correct monitors. If the issue was caused by a display monitor being off or on the wrong input, correct it, verify sector visibility, document, and stop.

steps.title: Check Whether Other Patients Are Affected
steps.instructions: Determine if this is isolated or system-wide. Action: Ask staff if other patients are missing from the central station. Compare the affected bed to nearby beds or another central monitoring station if available. Check whether the issue follows one patient, one bedside monitor, one room, one network jack, or the entire central station. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified. If multiple beds are missing, suspect a central station, network, server, or monitoring infrastructure issue and escalate appropriately.

steps.title: Restart Only When Clinically Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: Do not reboot a central monitoring station without understanding the patient safety impact. Action: Confirm clinical staff are actively monitoring patients at the bedside or through another approved central station. Follow site policy before restarting the Patient Information Center iX workstation or display. After restart, confirm the expected patient sectors return. Expected outcome: The central station reloads the correct patient sector layout and active monitoring data. If a restart restores the sector, document that the workstation or application display state was recovered.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Visibility After Recovery
steps.instructions: Do not stop troubleshooting once the sector visually returns. Action: Confirm the patient sector displays the correct bed label, patient information, waveforms, numerics, and alarm status. Ask clinical staff to verify the sector appears normal from their perspective. Confirm no patient is duplicated, missing, or assigned to the wrong sector. Expected outcome: The central station is displaying the correct active patient data. If the sector is restored and verified, return the central station to service and document the result.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips Patient Information Center iX was not showing the patient sector for Bed 12.
CCR.Cause: Found bedside monitor communicating normally, but the central station was on the incorrect unit layout and the expected bed sector was not visible.
CCR.Resolution: Restored the correct central station view, verified Bed 12 waveform and numerics were visible, and confirmed normal display with clinical staff.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or UPS power status checked.
Central workstation powered on and responsive.
Display monitor power and video cables checked.
Bedside monitor powered on and actively monitoring.
Network cable or wireless status verified.
Patient admit, discharge, transfer, or bed assignment checked.
Correct unit, care area, and sector layout verified.
Any communication messages or alarm behavior observed.
Environmental factors such as network jack, wireless signal, workstation location, or display configuration.
Indicator lights on workstation, display monitor, network connection, or bedside monitor.

title: Philips Patient Information Center iX Central Monitoring Station - Waveforms Not Displaying at Central Station
description: Troubleshooting missing bedside monitor waveforms at the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot central monitoring issues without confirming the patient is still being monitored locally. Verify that the bedside monitor is active, connected to the patient, and displaying waveforms locally. Confirm clinical staff can see alarms and waveforms at the bedside. If central monitoring is unavailable for an active patient, notify clinical staff immediately and follow the facility’s backup monitoring process. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely even if the central station is not displaying waveforms. Why it matters: A central station display issue should never leave staff assuming the patient is being monitored when waveform data is not visible. If this resolves the issue or confirms bedside monitoring is safe, continue only when it is appropriate to troubleshoot.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue affects one bed, multiple beds, or the entire central station. Check if the missing waveforms are limited to one patient bed. Check other beds on the same central station. Ask staff whether numeric values, alarms, and patient demographics are still visible. Expected outcome: One bed affected suggests a bedside monitor, network, assignment, or patient sector issue. Multiple beds affected suggests a central station, network, licensing, display layout, or server communication issue. If only one bed is affected, focus troubleshooting on that bedside monitor and its connection path.

steps.title: Check the Bedside Monitor First
steps.instructions: Verify that the bedside monitor is actually producing valid waveform data. At the bedside monitor, confirm that ECG, SpO₂, invasive pressure, respiration, or other expected waveforms are visible. Check that lead wires, sensors, transducers, and modules are connected. Confirm there are no “leads off,” “sensor off,” “module disconnected,” or similar messages. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor displays valid waveforms locally. If the bedside monitor does not show the waveform either, the central station is not the source of the issue. Troubleshoot the bedside monitor, patient cables, modules, or sensors first.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Is Assigned to the Correct Central Station Sector
steps.instructions: A waveform may not display if the bed is not assigned correctly or is being viewed in the wrong sector. Check that the correct bed label, room number, and patient name are assigned at the central station. Confirm the patient is admitted or associated correctly in the monitoring system. Verify that staff are viewing the correct central station screen, sector, or unit layout. Expected outcome: The correct patient bed appears in the expected central station location. If the bed was assigned incorrectly and correcting the assignment restores waveforms, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Whether Numerics Are Present Without Waveforms
steps.instructions: Determine whether the central station is receiving any data from the bedside monitor. Look for heart rate, SpO₂, blood pressure, respiratory rate, alarms, and patient demographics at the central station. Compare those values to the bedside monitor. Expected outcome: If numerics and alarms are present but waveforms are missing, the issue may be related to display layout, waveform selection, parameter priority, or central station configuration. If no data is present, suspect communication, network, bed assignment, or bedside monitor connectivity. This helps separate a display problem from a communication problem.

steps.title: Check Central Station Display Layout and Waveform Selection
steps.instructions: The waveform may be hidden, minimized, or replaced by another parameter. Review the patient sector layout. Check whether the waveform area is collapsed, hidden, or configured to show different parameters. Confirm the expected waveform, such as ECG lead II or pleth, is selected for display. Change the displayed waveform to another available waveform if appropriate. Expected outcome: The expected waveform appears in the patient sector. If waveform selection or layout correction restores the waveform, document the configuration issue and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Bedside Monitor Is Communicating on the Network
steps.instructions: Missing waveforms can occur if the bedside monitor is not communicating properly with the central station. Check the bedside monitor network connection. Verify Ethernet cables are fully seated if the monitor is hardwired. If wireless monitoring is used, check wireless signal status or connection indicators according to site workflow. Look for network or communication messages on the bedside monitor. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor shows normal network communication. If reconnecting a loose network cable restores waveform display, stop and document the connection issue.

steps.title: Check for Local Network or Port Issues
steps.instructions: If the bedside monitor appears connected but data is missing, rule out an external network path issue. Inspect the wall network jack and patch cable for damage. Swap with a known-good network cable if allowed by facility policy. Move the bedside monitor to a known-good network drop only if clinically safe and approved. Check whether another monitor works on the same network port, if practical. Expected outcome: The central station receives waveform data through a verified network path. If the issue follows the cable or network jack, escalate to IT/network support with the affected port, room, and device details.

steps.title: Check the Central Station Workstation Display
steps.instructions: Confirm the waveform is not missing due to a workstation display or software view issue. Check whether the central station mouse, keyboard, and display are responsive. Verify the correct monitoring application view is open. Check whether the issue appears on one central station display or all central station displays. Avoid rebooting a live central station unless clinical leadership approves and an alternate monitoring process is in place. Expected outcome: The central station display is active, responsive, and showing the correct patient monitoring layout. If the central station view was incorrect and restoring the correct view brings back waveforms, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Compare With Another Central Station or Review Station
steps.instructions: If available, compare the same patient bed from another authorized viewing location. Check whether the waveform appears on another central station, remote viewer, or review station. Compare patient data between the bedside monitor and alternate viewer. Expected outcome: If the waveform appears elsewhere, the issue is likely local to the affected central station workstation or layout. If the waveform is missing everywhere except the bedside monitor, suspect bedside network communication, system interface, or monitoring server communication.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue started after a known change. Ask staff whether the bed was recently moved, discharged, admitted, renamed, or transferred. Check whether the bedside monitor was swapped. Ask whether network work, server maintenance, software updates, or power events occurred recently. Expected outcome: Recent changes may explain a bed assignment, network, or configuration problem. If a recent room transfer or monitor swap caused the issue, correct the assignment or escalate to the team responsible for central monitoring configuration.

steps.title: Verify Alarm and Monitoring Status Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: Before considering the issue resolved, confirm the monitoring pathway is fully functional. Verify the expected waveform is displayed at the central station. Confirm numerics match the bedside monitor. Confirm alarms are active and visible according to clinical workflow. Have clinical staff verify that the patient appears correctly at the central station. Expected outcome: The central station displays the correct patient waveforms, numerics, and alarms. If confirmed, document the resolution and return the system to normal monitoring use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that waveforms from Bed 12 were not displaying at the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station.
CCR.Cause: Bedside monitor displayed waveforms locally, but the central station sector was assigned to the wrong bed label after a room transfer.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected the bed assignment at the central station, verified ECG and SpO₂ waveforms were visible, confirmed numerics matched the bedside monitor, and returned the bed to normal monitoring use.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or power status of the central station checked
Whether numerics were present at the central station
Whether one bed or multiple beds were affected
Network cable or wall jack checked
Any communication or network messages observed
Whether IT or Philips support was contacted

title: Philips Patient Information Center iX Central Monitoring Station - Alarms Not Displaying or Not Annunciating at Central Station
description: Troubleshooting missing, delayed, muted, or non-annunciating patient alarms at the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot alarm display or annunciation issues while relying on the affected central station as the only source of patient alarm notification. Confirm bedside monitoring is active at the patient location. Have clinical staff verify patient status directly at the bedside. Use local bedside monitor alarms, another central station, or alternate clinical surveillance until the issue is resolved. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues without depending on a central station that may not be displaying or sounding alarms. Why it matters: A central station alarm issue can create a serious patient safety risk if staff assume alarms are being received when they are not. If patient alarm visibility or annunciation cannot be confirmed, stop and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the problem is with alarm display, alarm sound, or both. Ask staff what they observed. Did the alarm occur at the bedside but not at the central station? Did the alarm display visually but not sound? Did the alarm appear late? Did only one bed fail to alarm? Are all beds affected? Check the central station display for active alarm indicators, alarm banners, patient sector status, and message areas. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a visual display problem, audio annunciation problem, delayed alarm communication, or bed-specific communication issue. Why it matters: “Alarms not working” can mean several different problems, and each has a different troubleshooting path.

steps.title: Confirm the Bedside Monitor Is Actually in Alarm
steps.instructions: Verify that the alarm condition is present at the source. Go to the bedside monitor and confirm the patient is assigned correctly. Confirm the parameter is being monitored. Confirm the alarm is active locally. Confirm alarm limits are enabled and appropriate. Confirm the monitor is not paused, silenced, suspended, in standby, or disconnected from the patient. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor shows an active alarm condition that should be sent to the central station. If the bedside monitor is not alarming locally, correct the bedside monitor setup first and stop. Why it matters: The central station cannot display or annunciate an alarm that was not generated by the bedside monitor.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Pause, Silence, or Audio Off Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the central station is not intentionally suppressing alarm audio. Check the Patient Information Center iX for alarm silence status. Check alarm pause status. Check audio off or muted speaker status. Check the volume setting. Check for any message indicating alarm suspension or alarm audio disabled. Confirm whether the issue affects only one patient sector or the entire central station. Expected outcome: Alarm audio is enabled, not paused, and not muted. If alarm annunciation returns after clearing pause, silence, mute, or volume settings, document the finding and stop. Why it matters: Alarm audio suppression can look like a failure even when the central station is receiving alarm data correctly.

steps.title: Verify Speaker and Audio Output
steps.instructions: Check whether the central station can produce sound at all. Confirm the workstation speaker, external speaker, or approved audio output is connected and powered. Check the Windows or workstation volume if accessible through approved hospital workflow. Verify the speaker cable is fully seated. Listen for other system sounds, advisory tones, or test tones if available. Inspect for disconnected, damaged, or loose audio cables. Expected outcome: The central station audio path is confirmed operational. If the alarm displays visually but no sounds are heard, and speaker or volume correction restores audio, return the device to service after functional verification. Why it matters: A disconnected or muted speaker can prevent annunciation even though alarm data is reaching the central station.

steps.title: Confirm Patient Sector Assignment and Visibility
steps.instructions: Verify the patient bed is assigned to the correct central station sector. Check that the correct bed label, patient name, room number, or monitor identifier appears in the expected sector. Confirm the sector is not blank, hidden, unassigned, or assigned to the wrong bed. Compare the bedside monitor label with the central station sector label. Expected outcome: The monitored patient is visible in the correct sector and associated with the correct bedside monitor. If the wrong bed or patient is assigned, correct the assignment through approved clinical or system workflow and verify alarms again. Why it matters: Alarm data may be active on the network but not displayed where staff expect it if the bed assignment is incorrect.

steps.title: Check Network and Bed Connection Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the central station is receiving live data from the bedside monitor. Look for signs of communication loss, such as no waveform data. Check for no numeric updates. Check for a bed disconnected message. Check for a blank patient sector. Check for stale values. Check for a network or communication warning. Compare the current bedside monitor readings to the central station readings. Expected outcome: Live patient data is updating at the central station. If the central station is not receiving live data, troubleshoot the bed connection or network path before focusing on alarms. Why it matters: Alarm display and annunciation depend on reliable communication between the bedside monitor and central station.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is One Bed or Multiple Beds
steps.instructions: Determine the scope of the failure. Ask staff whether alarms are missing from one room, one unit, or all monitored beds. Compare several patient sectors. Check whether other central stations on the unit are receiving and annunciating alarms normally. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as bed-specific, central-station-specific, or wider network/system related. If only one bed is affected, focus on that bedside monitor, network port, cable, monitor assignment, and sector configuration. If multiple beds are affected, escalate as a possible central station, network, server, or alarm configuration issue. Why it matters: Scope determines whether this is a local bedside issue or a broader monitoring infrastructure problem.

steps.title: Inspect Network Cabling and External Connections
steps.instructions: Check easily verifiable physical connections before assuming software or server failure. Inspect the central station network cable and wall jack. Confirm the cable is fully seated and not damaged. Check link lights if visible on the network port or workstation. Do not unplug active monitoring network connections unless approved and safe to do so. If a bedside monitor is hardwired, check the bedside network cable and wall connection as well. Expected outcome: External network connections are secure and undamaged. If reseating or replacing an approved external cable restores alarm communication, verify alarm display and annunciation before returning the system to service. Why it matters: Loose or damaged network connections can cause missing patient data and missing alarms.

steps.title: Check Alarm Configuration and Unit Workflow Settings
steps.instructions: Verify the alarm behavior is not caused by configuration or workflow setup. Review approved alarm settings with clinical leadership or the monitoring system administrator. Confirm alarm limits are enabled. Confirm alarm priorities are not configured differently than expected. Confirm the patient is not in a mode that suppresses certain alarms. Confirm the central station is configured to display and annunciate that bed’s alarms. Confirm no recent configuration change was made. Expected outcome: Alarm behavior matches expected unit configuration. If settings are incorrect, correct them through approved hospital procedures and verify alarm function. Why it matters: Alarm routing and annunciation can depend on configuration, patient assignment, priority, and unit-specific alarm policies.

steps.title: Check for Workstation Freeze or Software Responsiveness
steps.instructions: Confirm the Patient Information Center iX workstation is operating normally. Observe whether the display is updating. Check if the mouse, keyboard, touchscreen, or user interface responds. Confirm waveforms and numerics are refreshing. Look for application errors, frozen screens, delayed response, or abnormal pop-up messages. Expected outcome: The central station interface is responsive and patient data is current. If the workstation appears frozen, do not rely on it for patient monitoring. Remove it from service and escalate for Clinical Engineering, IT, or Philips support. Why it matters: A frozen central station may appear normal at a glance while failing to update alarms or patient data.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Verification
steps.instructions: After external checks or corrections, verify alarm display and annunciation using an approved safe method. Coordinate with clinical staff and follow hospital policy. Do not create unsafe patient conditions to test alarms. Use an approved simulator, test patient setup, or controlled bedside monitor alarm test if available. Confirm the alarm occurs at the bedside monitor. Confirm the alarm displays at the central station. Confirm the alarm annunciates audibly at the central station. Confirm the alarm appears in the correct patient sector. Confirm the alarm clears correctly when the condition resolves. Expected outcome: Alarm display and annunciation are confirmed before the central station is trusted again for clinical monitoring. If alarms still do not display or sound correctly, remove the affected central station or bed connection from service and escalate. Why it matters: Alarm systems must be functionally verified after troubleshooting, not assumed fixed.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing staff reported that alarms from a monitored patient were not displaying or sounding at the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station.
CCR.Cause: Bedside monitor alarmed locally, but the central station sector showed no active alarm due to a communication or assignment issue.
CCR.Resolution: Verified bedside alarm operation, checked patient sector assignment and network status, restored central station alarm display, and confirmed audible annunciation with staff before returning to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether one bed or multiple beds were involved
Whether central station visual alarms were missing
Alarm pause, silence, mute, or volume status
Whether waveforms and numerics were updating
Whether another central station showed the alarm correctly
Any recent configuration or network changes
Environmental factors around the central station or workstation
Indicator lights or network link light status

title: Philips Patient Information Center iX Central Monitoring Station - Cannot Print or Review Patient Data
description: Troubleshooting missing print, review, or stored patient data functions from the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the central station in a way that delays active patient monitoring. Confirm bedside monitors are still displaying patient waveforms, numerics, and alarms. Verify staff can monitor the patient locally at the bedside if central review or printing is unavailable. If central monitoring display is also affected, escalate immediately and use unit downtime procedures. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains active even if printing or review functions are unavailable. Why it matters: Printing and review issues are usually secondary to live monitoring, but they can affect documentation, rhythm review, and clinical workflow.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what function is failing. Ask staff whether the issue is cannot print strips or reports. Ask staff whether the issue is cannot open patient review. Ask staff whether the issue is cannot view stored waveforms, alarms, or trends. Ask staff whether the issue is cannot print for one patient only. Ask staff whether the issue is cannot print or review any patient data. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to printing, review access, patient-specific data, or a wider central station problem. If the issue is only one patient sector, focus on that patient’s admission, connection, and data availability before assuming a printer or system failure.

steps.title: Confirm Live Patient Data Is Present
steps.instructions: Check whether the patient is actively connected and sending data to the central station. Verify the patient sector shows current waveforms, heart rate, SpO2, NBP, or other active parameters. Confirm the bedside monitor is powered on, assigned to the correct patient, and communicating with the central station. Check for messages such as no data, disconnected, not admitted, equipment offline, or communication loss. Expected outcome: Live patient data is visible before attempting review or printing. If live data is missing, troubleshoot the bed connection or bedside monitor communication first. Stop this guide and follow the no-data or bed-not-connected workflow.

steps.title: Check Patient Admission and Location Assignment
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is admitted or assigned correctly in the system. Verify the patient name, room, bed label, and MRN are correct if available. Check whether the patient was recently transferred, discharged, readmitted, or moved to another bed. Confirm staff are selecting the correct patient sector when attempting review or print. Expected outcome: The central station is looking at the correct patient record and bed assignment. Why it matters: Review and print functions may appear unavailable if the wrong patient, discharged patient, or inactive bed is selected.

steps.title: Confirm There Is Data Available to Review
steps.instructions: Verify that enough monitoring time has passed for reviewable data to exist. Ask when the patient was connected or admitted. Check whether the event, alarm, trend, or waveform review window contains stored data. Confirm staff are not trying to review a time period before the patient was connected. Expected outcome: The selected time range contains stored patient data. If the patient was just admitted or reconnected, allow time for new data to populate before assuming a failure.

steps.title: Check Review Filters and Time Range
steps.instructions: Incorrect review settings can make stored data appear missing. Check the selected time range, patient, alarm/event category, and waveform/trend view. Reset the review window to a recent time period where the patient was known to be monitored. Try viewing a different review category, such as alarms, trends, or waveforms. Expected outcome: Previously hidden data becomes visible when the correct review range or category is selected. If changing filters restores the information, educate staff and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check User Access or Workflow Restrictions
steps.instructions: Some functions may depend on login status, permissions, or hospital configuration. Confirm whether the user is logged in, if applicable. Have an authorized staff member attempt the same print or review function. Check whether the issue occurs for one user or all users. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either user-specific or system-wide. If only one user is affected, escalate through the normal IT or application support process for access review.

steps.title: Check Printer Power and Local Printer Status
steps.instructions: If review works but printing fails, check the printer first. Verify the printer is powered on. Check for paper, toner, paper jam, open tray, warning lights, or offline status. Confirm the printer display does not show an error condition. Print a test page if allowed by site procedure. Expected outcome: The printer is ready and able to print. If the printer has a paper, toner, jam, or offline issue, correct it or route to the responsible printer support team. Stop if printing is restored.

steps.title: Check Printer Connection
steps.instructions: Verify the printer is connected to the central station or network as expected. Check USB, Ethernet, or network connections depending on the local setup. Confirm cables are fully seated and not damaged. If using a network printer, verify the printer has link lights at the network port. Do not move network cables between ports unless approved by site procedure. Expected outcome: The printer has a valid physical connection. Why it matters: A loose cable or disconnected network drop can cause printing failure while the central station itself continues to function normally.

steps.title: Try Printing From Another Patient or Central Station Function
steps.instructions: Determine whether the problem is patient-specific or printer/system-wide. Attempt to print a strip, report, or review page from another monitored patient if clinically appropriate. Try printing a different type of report if available. Ask whether other central stations can print normally. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to one patient record, one function, one central station, or the shared printer path. If only one patient is affected, continue checking that patient’s data and assignment. If all patients are affected, focus on printer, network, or central station configuration.

steps.title: Check for Central Station Application Freezing or Delays
steps.instructions: Review and printing may fail if the application is slow, frozen, or partially unresponsive. Confirm whether the mouse, keyboard, touchscreen, or menu selections respond normally. Check whether other patient sectors update in real time. Look for application alerts, communication warnings, or system messages. Do not restart the central station without following site procedure and confirming patient monitoring coverage. Expected outcome: The central station application is responsive and updating. If the central station is frozen or not updating patient data, remove it from active reliance and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Confirm Network and Server-Dependent Functions
steps.instructions: Patient review and print functions may depend on hospital network services, central monitoring servers, or data storage. Check whether multiple Philips central stations are having the same review or printing issue. Ask whether the issue started after network work, server maintenance, power interruption, or software downtime. Verify other network-dependent functions, such as patient lookup or central data access, if available. Expected outcome: A wider infrastructure issue is identified or ruled out. If multiple central stations are affected, escalate to Clinical Engineering, IT, and Philips support according to site procedure.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only If Safe and Allowed
steps.instructions: A restart may clear a local application issue, but only when patient monitoring coverage is maintained. Confirm no active patient safety risk will be created by restarting the central station. Ensure bedside monitoring remains available. Notify clinical staff before restarting. Follow site-approved shutdown and startup procedures. After restart, verify live data, review access, and printing. Expected outcome: The central station returns to normal operation and review/print functions are restored. If the restart resolves the issue, document the behavior and final verification.

steps.title: Verify Final Operation
steps.instructions: Before returning the system to normal use, confirm both review and printing if applicable. Open patient review for an active patient. Confirm stored data is visible for an appropriate time range. Send a test print or clinically appropriate print job. Verify the printed output is complete, legible, and associated with the correct patient. Expected outcome: Patient data can be reviewed and printed correctly. If successful, return the device to service and document the work order.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips Patient Information Center iX central station could not print strips or review stored patient data.
CCR.Cause: Verified live patient monitoring was present, but review and print functions failed after checking patient assignment, printer status, and network connection.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the central station from active reliance, labeled it Out of Service, and escalated for Philips/IT evaluation after external causes were ruled out.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or any alarm-related messages displayed
Accessories swapped, including printer, USB cable, Ethernet cable, mouse, keyboard, or connected peripherals
Power behavior, including outlet status, UPS status, restart performed or not performed, and whether the central station was responsive or frozen
Environmental factors, including recent network work, server maintenance, power interruption, software downtime, or unit workflow changes
Indicator lights, including printer warning lights, network link lights, and any central station status indicators
Final device status, including returned to service, removed from service, labeled Out of Service, or escalated for Philips/IT evaluation

title: Philips BV Pulsera C-Arm - System Fails to Boot or Application Does Not Load
description: Troubleshooting BV Pulsera startup failures, boot errors, frozen loading screens, or application launch problems before escalating for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot a boot failure during an active procedure unless patient care has already been safely transferred or the procedure has been paused. Action: Confirm with the clinical team that the C-arm is not actively needed for imaging. If imaging is required, move the case to another available C-arm or imaging system. Do not repeatedly power-cycle the system during a procedure without coordinating with the clinical team. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on a C-arm that cannot complete startup. Why it matters: A C-arm that does not boot or load its application cannot be trusted for live imaging, dose control, image storage, or procedure support.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Startup Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the system is doing during startup. Action: Observe the boot sequence from power-on. Check whether the issue is no power at all. Check whether power lights are on but there is no display. Check whether the Philips startup screen appears but freezes. Check whether the application begins loading but never opens. Check whether an error message appears during boot. Check whether the system reaches the operating interface but imaging functions are unavailable. Check whether the workstation or monitor cart boots differently than the C-arm stand. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or internal faults are suspected. If the issue is only a delayed startup and the application loads normally after a reasonable time, document the behavior and return the system to service only after functional checks pass.

steps.title: Check AC Power at the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Verify that the BV Pulsera is receiving proper facility power. Action: Confirm the power cord is connected firmly to the wall outlet. Test the outlet with an outlet tester or known-good device. Check for a tripped breaker, switched outlet, loose receptacle, or damaged outlet. If the system is plugged into a power strip or extension cord, remove that setup and connect directly to an approved wall outlet if allowed by facility policy. Expected outcome: The C-arm receives stable AC power from a verified outlet. If the system boots normally after correcting the outlet or power source, perform a functional check, document the cause, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Main Power Cord and Connection Points
steps.instructions: A loose or damaged power cord can cause incomplete startup, intermittent shutdown, or failure to load the application. Action: Inspect the power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, exposed conductors, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the system connection point if detachable. Gently check for looseness at the connector without stressing the cord. Look for burn marks, unusual heat, or electrical smell near the plug or inlet. Expected outcome: The power cord and inlet appear safe and secure. If cord damage, heat, burning smell, or arcing is found, remove the system from service immediately. Do not continue troubleshooting on live power.

steps.title: Confirm System Power Switches Are in the Correct Position
steps.instructions: The BV Pulsera may have separate power controls depending on configuration. Action: Check the main power switch on the system. Verify the monitor cart, workstation, or viewing station is powered on if separate. Confirm any emergency power-off or service switch has not been activated. Check for illuminated power indicators on the C-arm stand and monitor cart. Expected outcome: All required system components are powered correctly. If a power switch or disconnected monitor cart power source caused the issue, correct it, restart the system, complete functional testing, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Check the Interconnect Cables Between the C-Arm and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: A communication or video/data connection problem can look like a boot or application failure. Action: Inspect the system interconnect cable between the C-arm stand and monitor cart. Confirm connectors are fully seated and locked if applicable. Look for bent pins, cracked connector shells, pulled cable strain relief, crushed cable sections, or damage from rolling over the cable. Reseat external connectors only if safe and accessible. Expected outcome: The C-arm and monitor cart have secure external communication and video/data connections. If reseating a loose external cable allows the application to load normally, perform imaging and display checks, document the correction, and stop.

steps.title: Check Display and Monitor Behavior
steps.instructions: The application may be loading, but the display may not be showing it correctly. Action: Confirm monitors are powered on. Check brightness and input/source selection if adjustable. Look for backlight activity, splash screen, cursor, error text, or frozen image. Compare behavior between the live monitor and reference monitor if the system has more than one display. Check for loose external video cables if accessible. Expected outcome: Display failure is separated from true system boot failure. If the system appears to boot but the monitor remains blank, suspect display, video cable, monitor power, or video output issues rather than a full system startup failure. Remove from service if imaging cannot be safely viewed.

steps.title: Remove Nonessential External Accessories
steps.instructions: External peripherals can occasionally interfere with startup. Action: Disconnect nonessential accessories such as USB devices, printers, external storage devices, network cables, or noncritical peripheral connections if permitted by facility procedure. Do not remove required system interconnects or safety-related cables. Restart the system after removing nonessential accessories. Expected outcome: The system boots without interference from external accessories. If the system boots after removing a peripheral, reconnect items one at a time to identify the cause. Document the failed accessory or connection and stop once confirmed.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction or Locked System State
steps.instructions: Some mobile imaging systems may stop startup or application readiness if components are not in an expected state. Action: Confirm the C-arm is parked safely. Check that brakes, wheels, and mechanical locks are not causing obvious system stress or cable strain. Inspect for collision damage, loose panels, or signs the unit was recently dropped, hit, or moved roughly. Ask staff whether the system failed after transport, cleaning, a collision, or a previous shutdown. Expected outcome: External mechanical damage or transport-related causes are identified before internal failure is assumed. If physical damage is found, remove the system from service and escalate for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Only perform a controlled restart after basic external power and cable checks are complete. Action: Shut the system down using the normal method if the interface is available. If the application is frozen and normal shutdown is not possible, follow facility-approved power-down practice. Wait briefly before restoring power. Power the system back on and observe the full boot sequence. Expected outcome: The system completes startup and loads the application normally. If the system boots successfully, perform a basic functional check before returning it to service. Verify image display, system readiness, movement controls, and any required safety indicators.

steps.title: Record Any Error Messages or Boot Screen Behavior
steps.instructions: If the system still fails, capture useful information before escalation. Action: Document the exact error message if shown. Record whether the failure occurs before or after the Philips splash screen. Note whether the issue affects the C-arm stand, monitor cart, application software, display, or all components. Take a photo of the screen if allowed by facility policy and no patient information is visible. Check for unusual fan noise, repeated clicking, beeping, heat, or burning smell. Expected outcome: Repair support has clear failure details instead of a vague “will not boot” report.

steps.title: Perform Basic Post-Boot Functional Checks if Startup Recovers
steps.instructions: If the BV Pulsera eventually boots normally, do not return it to service based on startup alone. Action: Confirm the application loads completely. Verify the user interface responds normally. Confirm image display is present. Check that the system can enter imaging-ready status. Verify no startup, generator, detector, communication, or storage errors remain active. Confirm date/time and system configuration appear reasonable. Expected outcome: The system is safe to return to service only if startup and basic functionality are normal. If the problem returns, becomes intermittent, or the system fails any functional check, remove it from service and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips BV Pulsera powered on but the application would not load past the startup screen.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall power and external connections were good, but the system repeatedly froze during application startup.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-arm from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented startup behavior, and escalated for imaging repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the Philips startup screen appears
Whether application freezes, loops, or never opens
Any unusual sounds, heat, odor, or fan behavior
Whether the issue occurred after transport or cleaning
Whether a controlled restart changed the behavior

title: Philips BV Pulsera C-Arm - No X-ray / Exposure Not Permitted
description: Troubleshooting when the BV Pulsera will not allow X-ray exposure due to safety, connection, setup, or system readiness conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the C-arm is not needed for an active procedure. Confirm with the OR team whether the system failed before, during, or after a case. If a patient is present, make sure imaging needs are covered by another available C-arm if necessary. Do not repeatedly attempt exposure without understanding why the system is preventing X-ray. Confirm radiation safety practices are followed before any test exposure. Expected outcome: Patient care and staff radiation safety are protected before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: “Exposure not permitted” is often a protective lockout. Forcing repeated attempts without confirming the cause can delay care or create unsafe workflow.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Reported Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm what the system is actually doing when exposure is requested. Check whether the system displays a message such as: Exposure not permitted, No X-ray, X-ray inhibited, System not ready, Door, brake, footswitch, hand switch, generator, or tube-related warning. Ask staff whether the issue occurs with the hand switch, footswitch, or both. Observe whether the system beeps, displays an error, or does nothing when exposure is attempted. Expected outcome: You confirm whether the problem is a true exposure lockout, a switch/accessory issue, or a broader system readiness fault. If the issue is only with one exposure control, continue checking that control before suspecting the generator.

steps.title: Confirm the System Is Fully Booted and Ready
steps.instructions: Exposure may be blocked if the BV Pulsera application has not fully loaded or the system is not in a ready state. Verify the workstation and C-arm have completed startup. Check that the imaging application is open and responsive. Confirm the monitor displays the expected imaging screen rather than a startup, login, error, service, or standby screen. Wait for any initialization messages to clear. Expected outcome: The system reaches normal operating mode and allows preparation for exposure. If the system was still initializing and exposure becomes available after startup completes, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: An active emergency stop or safety interlock can prevent X-ray exposure. Inspect emergency stop buttons on the system, workstation, or relevant control areas. Confirm no emergency stop is pressed or latched. Release any engaged emergency stop according to normal hospital procedure. Power cycle the system if required after releasing the emergency stop. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the safety interlock as cleared. If exposure is restored after clearing an emergency stop, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify Power and Mains Connection
steps.instructions: Low, unstable, or missing mains power can prevent generator readiness. Confirm the system is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Verify the power cord is fully seated and not damaged. Check that the outlet has power. Avoid using extension cords or questionable power strips. Confirm the system is not relying on an unstable power source. Expected outcome: The C-arm has stable AC power and can support generator operation. If correcting the power connection restores X-ray readiness, document the outlet or power issue and stop.

steps.title: Check the Hand Switch and Footswitch Connections
steps.instructions: The system may not permit exposure if the exposure control is disconnected, damaged, or not recognized. Inspect the hand switch cable and connector. Inspect the footswitch cable and connector if used. Look for bent pins, loose connections, crushed cable sections, fluid contamination, or physical damage. Disconnect and reconnect the exposure control securely if appropriate. Test exposure readiness using the alternate control if available and safe. Expected outcome: The system recognizes a valid exposure control. If one switch works and the other does not, remove the failed switch from service and document the accessory failure.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Operating Mode and Exam Setup
steps.instructions: Some systems will not allow exposure if the exam, mode, or imaging setup is incomplete. Confirm an exam or patient/session is selected if required by site workflow. Verify the system is not in review-only, standby, service, shutdown, or non-imaging mode. Check that fluoroscopy or radiography mode is selected appropriately. Confirm no on-screen prompt is waiting for user acknowledgement. Expected outcome: The system is placed in a valid imaging mode where exposure is allowed. If selecting the proper mode restores exposure, document the setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Check C-Arm and Workstation Communication
steps.instructions: The mobile viewing station and C-arm must communicate properly for imaging operation. Confirm the C-arm and workstation are connected as normally configured. Inspect visible communication cables between system components if applicable. Check for loose, damaged, or disconnected connectors. Confirm the workstation display updates normally when system settings are changed. Expected outcome: The workstation and C-arm appear synchronized and responsive. If communication is restored after reseating an external connection, document the connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Check for Tube, Generator, or Cooling-Related Messages
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the tube or generator is overheated, not ready, or reporting a fault. Look for messages related to tube heat, generator readiness, cooling, overload, or exposure inhibit. Ask staff whether the system had been used heavily before the failure. Allow appropriate cooling time if the system indicates tube heat or thermal limitation. Do not continue attempting exposures during a thermal lockout. Expected outcome: Temporary heat-related lockouts clear after cooling, or a persistent generator/tube fault is identified. If the message clears after cooling and exposure is restored, document the thermal condition and final status.

steps.title: Inspect Mechanical Position and Brakes
steps.instructions: Some C-arm conditions may prevent exposure if the system is not positioned, locked, or detected correctly. Verify the C-arm is positioned safely. Check that required locks, brakes, or movement controls are not creating an abnormal system state. Confirm no collision, obstruction, or abnormal mechanical condition is present. Listen for unusual sounds from the C-arm during positioning. Expected outcome: No obvious mechanical or positioning issue is preventing imaging. If repositioning or correcting a brake/lock condition restores exposure, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Dose, Collimator, and Imaging Settings
steps.instructions: Extreme or invalid imaging settings can contribute to exposure problems. Review the selected imaging mode, dose level, collimator position, and field setup. Confirm settings appear reasonable for normal test imaging. Reset to a known standard exam or default setup if permitted by site procedure. Avoid changing clinical protocol settings without confirming with authorized staff. Expected outcome: Imaging settings are valid and do not prevent exposure. If correcting the setup restores X-ray, document the setting-related cause and stop.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System Only When Safe
steps.instructions: A controlled restart may clear a temporary software or readiness state. Confirm the system is not actively needed for a patient. Shut down the BV Pulsera using the normal shutdown process. Wait briefly before powering back on. Allow the system to fully boot. Confirm whether exposure is now permitted. Expected outcome: Temporary software or startup state issues are cleared. If the system works normally after restart, perform a basic functional check and document the restart resolution.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: After any corrective action, verify the system is safe and functional before returning it to service. Use approved test objects or site procedure for a non-patient test exposure. Confirm the system permits exposure. Verify image appears on the monitor. Check that fluoro activation indicators and audible signals behave normally. Confirm no active error messages remain. Expected outcome: X-ray exposure is permitted, image acquisition works, and the system is ready for clinical use. If the functional check passes, return the unit to service according to department policy.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported that the Philips BV Pulsera would not allow X-ray exposure and displayed an exposure not permitted condition during setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the footswitch connection loose at the C-arm, preventing the system from recognizing the exposure control.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the footswitch connector, verified exposure readiness, completed a test image, and returned the C-arm to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or exact message displayed
Accessories swapped, including hand switch or footswitch
Power behavior, outlet tested, power cord condition, and system boot status
Environmental factors such as heavy use, heat, cooling, fluid exposure, or unusual smell
Indicator lights, fluoro activation indicators, audible signals, generator messages, tube heat messages, or cooling messages
Final device status, including whether restart changed the condition and whether the functional test passed

title: Philips BV Pulsera C-Arm - Image Intensifier Or Camera Image Quality Issue
description: Troubleshooting poor image quality caused by positioning, display, cable, image intensifier, camera, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot image quality problems during an active procedure unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Confirm whether the C-arm is actively being used on a patient. If image quality is affecting clinical decision-making, have staff switch to another imaging system if available. Do not continue using the unit clinically if anatomy, instruments, contrast, or landmarks cannot be clearly visualized. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before technical troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: Poor image quality can lead to missed anatomy, incorrect positioning, longer procedure time, or unnecessary radiation exposure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Image Quality Problem
steps.instructions: Ask the user to describe the issue clearly. Determine whether the complaint is: blurred image, dark or washed-out image, noisy or grainy image, image lag, artifacts, poor contrast, shading or uneven brightness, distorted image, camera/I.I. alignment concern, or image only poor on one monitor. Expected outcome: The symptom is clearly identified before parts or internal failures are assumed. If the image problem is not present during testing, document that the issue could not be duplicated and ask staff what mode, position, or procedure type was being used.

steps.title: Check Monitor Display and Viewing Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the issue is not only a display problem. Check both monitors if the system has dual displays. Verify brightness and contrast settings are reasonable. Look for one monitor appearing worse than the other. Check for loose monitor video cables, damaged connectors, or obvious display defects. Expected outcome: The image should appear similar on both displays. If only one monitor is affected, troubleshoot the monitor or video path before suspecting the image intensifier or camera. If correcting monitor settings or cabling resolves the issue, return the unit to service and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the C-Arm Positioning and Field of View
steps.instructions: Poor positioning can look like an imaging failure. Confirm the anatomy or test object is centered in the image field. Check that the image intensifier is not too far from the object. Verify the X-ray tube and image intensifier are properly aligned. Remove unnecessary objects from the beam path. Expected outcome: The test object should appear centered, stable, and reasonably sharp. If repositioning improves the image, the issue was likely setup-related. Return the unit to service if image quality is acceptable.

steps.title: Check for Objects or Materials in the Beam Path
steps.instructions: External items can cause artifacts or poor contrast. Look for metal objects, lead shielding, table rails, thick mattress pads, surgical instruments, cables crossing the field, and positioning devices. Expected outcome: Removing unnecessary material should improve image clarity and contrast. If the image improves after clearing the beam path, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Test Image Quality With a Simple Known Object
steps.instructions: Use a controlled test to separate clinical setup issues from equipment issues. Place a known object or approved image quality test tool in the field. Acquire an image using normal fluoroscopy settings. Compare the image to expected appearance for sharpness, brightness, contrast, and artifacts.
