CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench repair. Printer replacement recommended; printer data cable and processor communication path to be evaluated if communication failure remains after printer replacement.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Incompatible Printer
description: Troubleshooting the Incompatible Printer error on a Philips HeartStart MRx when the installed printer hardware does not match the expected software configuration, affecting printing, readiness checks, and documentation confidence.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if the printer error affects documentation, event recording, readiness checks, or confidence in device operation. Replace it with another ready defibrillator before troubleshooting if active patient care is involved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the device on and confirm the exact displayed error is Incompatible Printer. Note when the error appears and whether the printer operates, fails to print, or prevents readiness checks.

steps.title: Confirm the Installed Printer Type
steps.instructions: Inspect the installed printer assembly and identify whether a 50 mm or 75 mm printer is installed. Compare the installed printer width to the device configuration or facility standard.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service or Parts Replacement
steps.instructions: Review whether the printer assembly was recently replaced, swapped, or installed after repair. Check service records or parts documentation if available.

steps.title: Verify Software Configuration
steps.instructions: Check the device configuration to confirm what printer type the software expects. Confirm that the configured printer type matches the installed printer hardware.

steps.title: Check External Printer-Related Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer door, paper roll, paper seating, printer opening, debris, jammed paper, liquid intrusion, and impact damage to rule out separate external printer faults.

steps.title: Match the Hardware to the Expected Configuration
steps.instructions: If the software expects a 75 mm printer, verify that the correct 75 mm printer assembly is installed. Correct any hardware or configuration mismatch using approved service procedures.

steps.title: Retest the Device
steps.instructions: After confirming or correcting the printer hardware and configuration match, power cycle the device, run the operational check, and attempt a test print if allowed by facility procedure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the correct printer assembly is installed and the error no longer appears, confirm final device status, printer function, test print result, and readiness check result before returning the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Incompatible Printer error during startup and printer function could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Installed printer type did not match the printer type expected by the device software configuration.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service, verified printer type and configuration, and sent unit for correction of printer hardware/software mismatch before operational check and test print.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: SW Watchdog Fail
description: Troubleshooting SW Watchdog Fail errors on the Philips HeartStart MRx that may cause restart behavior, lockups, software instability, failed operational checks, or unreliable defibrillator operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if SW Watchdog Fail appears during operation, startup, charging, monitoring, pacing, defibrillation readiness checks, or operational testing. Provide a known-good backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm the exact displayed error is SW Watchdog Fail. Note whether it appears during startup, self-test, monitoring, charging, therapy mode selection, normal operation, or after restart.

steps.title: Translate the Error Into the Real-World Failure
steps.instructions: Treat the complaint as a software stability or system control issue. Ask whether the unit restarted unexpectedly, froze, became unresponsive, failed startup, or failed an operational check.

steps.title: Restart the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Allow startup and self-check behavior to complete. If the error returns, treat it as recurring.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Verify known-good AC power, normal AC indicator behavior, proper battery seating, and adequate battery charge. Test with a known-good compatible battery if available.

steps.title: Inspect External Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the housing, display, controls, battery bay, AC input area, external ports, and therapy selector for damage, contamination, loose connectors, fluid intrusion, or evidence of impact.

steps.title: Remove Nonessential Accessories and Recheck
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential accessories such as ECG cable, SpO2 sensor, NBP hose, CO2 accessories, therapy cable, printer-related accessories, data card, or communication accessories. Restart and check whether the error returns.

steps.title: Run Available Operational Checks
steps.instructions: If the unit powers on successfully, perform the facility-approved operational check. Verify power, display, controls, therapy selector, battery indicators, AC indicators, accessory recognition if applicable, and absence of SW Watchdog Fail.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Error Is Recurring
steps.instructions: Classify the error as recurring if it returns after restart, appears during multiple power cycles, occurs during operational check, appears intermittently after clearing, or staff report repeated restarts or lockups.

steps.title: Reload Software if Approved and Supported
steps.instructions: If the error recurs, follow the approved Philips HeartStart MRx software support process used by the facility. Only trained and authorized personnel should perform software reloads using approved tools and version control.

steps.title: Verify After Software Reload
steps.instructions: After approved software reload, restart the device and complete required operational checks. Confirm the error does not return, startup is normal, controls respond, monitoring and therapy readiness pass, and configuration is correct.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported SW Watchdog Fail error during startup. Staff removed the unit from service due to concern for unreliable defibrillator operation.
CCR.Cause: SW Watchdog Fail confirmed during troubleshooting. AC power, battery seating, visible damage, and accessory-related causes were checked. Error returned after restart, indicating recurring software watchdog failure requiring approved software reload or Philips escalation.
CCR.Resolution: Restarted unit, checked AC power and battery seating, inspected external condition, and removed nonessential accessories. Error recurred after restart. Unit labeled Out of Service and escalated for approved software reload or Philips Response Center support.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Replace Clock Battery
description: Troubleshooting the Replace Clock Battery error on the Philips HeartStart MRx when date/time settings may not be retained and event documentation accuracy may be affected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the defibrillator from patient care if the error is active or if date/time accuracy cannot be trusted. Provide clinical staff with a ready backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the HeartStart MRx and confirm the exact Replace Clock Battery message. Check when the message appears and whether the device loses date/time settings.

steps.title: Check Main Power and Main Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the main battery is installed, charged, and accepted by the device. Connect AC power if available and confirm external power is recognized.

steps.title: Confirm Date and Time Settings
steps.instructions: Check whether the date and time are incorrect, reset, or blank. Correct the date/time if needed, power cycle the device, and verify whether the settings are retained.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious External or Handling Issues
steps.instructions: Check for physical damage, fluid intrusion, cracked housing, drop evidence, battery bay contamination, or external power connection damage.

steps.title: Determine Whether Service Is Required
steps.instructions: If the device powers normally but displays Replace Clock Battery, treat the issue as a service-level internal clock backup battery condition and remove the device from clinical use.

steps.title: Verify Correction After Approved Service
steps.instructions: After qualified service is completed, set the correct date/time and verify that the Replace Clock Battery message clears and date/time is retained after power cycling.

steps.title: Run Required Operational Checks
steps.instructions: Perform required post-service checks. Verify normal startup, correct date/time retention, acceptable battery status, recognized AC power, no additional errors, and passing operational checks.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the clock battery condition has been corrected, date/time is retained, and required checks pass, document the work and return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Replace Clock Battery error. Staff reported date/time issue after device startup.
CCR.Cause: Replace Clock Battery error confirmed. Date/time retention issue observed after power cycle. Main battery and AC power were checked with no external power issue found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service. Clock backup battery condition corrected through approved service process. Reset date/time, verified time retention after power cycle, completed required operational checks, and returned unit to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printer temperature is too high
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 printer over-temperature error that can stop or limit ECG report and rhythm strip printing.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PageWriter TC70 is needed for active ECG acquisition or printing, provide another verified ECG machine before troubleshooting. Do not continue clinical use if the device cannot reliably print required ECG reports.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Printer temperature is too high. Observe when the error appears and whether the printer feeds paper, produces incomplete printouts, or disables printing.

steps.title: Stop Printing and Power Down the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: Shut down the PageWriter TC70 and leave it powered off for at least 20 minutes to allow the print head to cool.

steps.title: Check the Environment Around the Unit
steps.instructions: Confirm the cardiograph is not near a heat source, direct sunlight, heating vent, or enclosed space with poor airflow. Move it to a normal clinical environment if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the Printer Area Externally
steps.instructions: After cooldown, inspect the printer door, paper drawer, and paper path for jams, wrinkled paper, debris, paper drag, binding, or a printer door that is not fully closed.

steps.title: Verify Correct Paper Loading
steps.instructions: Open the printer paper drawer and confirm the paper is seated correctly, not backward, folded, jammed, or too tight in the tray. Close the drawer fully.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: Power the PageWriter TC70 back on after the cooldown and watch for the printer temperature error during startup.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Test Print
steps.instructions: Print a short ECG report or test rhythm strip in a Biomed-controlled setting. Avoid repeated long print runs during the first test.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Heavy Printing Conditions
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the error occurred after repeated ECGs, continuous rhythm printing, or multiple long reports.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after shutdown, cooldown, paper path inspection, and a successful test print, return the cardiograph to service and document the final device status.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed Printer temperature is too high. Staff reported printing was unavailable after ECG or rhythm printing.
CCR.Cause: Print head over-temperature condition observed. Printer area inspected for paper jam, paper loading issue, restricted paper path, and environmental heat exposure. Error cleared after shutdown and 20-minute cooldown.
CCR.Resolution: Powered down cardiograph for 20 minutes, inspected printer drawer and paper path, verified paper was loaded correctly, restarted unit, and completed successful test print. Device returned to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printer underflow
description: Troubleshooting Printer underflow errors on the Philips PageWriter TC70 that may interrupt rhythm printing and indicate intermittent PIM communication or signal acquisition problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop using the affected cardiograph on an active patient and move ECG acquisition to another verified device if clinically needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact Printer underflow message and identify whether it occurs during rhythm printing, ECG acquisition, PIM movement, or patient cable movement.

steps.title: Check Whether Printing Stops, Pauses, or Produces Poor Output
steps.instructions: Perform rhythm printing in a controlled setting and look for interrupted printing, blank sections, delayed waveform output, or ECG signal dropout.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Connect the cardiograph to known-good AC power and confirm there are no low battery or power-related warnings.

steps.title: Inspect the Printer Area for Simple Issues
steps.instructions: Check paper loading, paper binding, torn paper, debris, and printer drawer or door closure before repeating the rhythm print test.

steps.title: Check the PIM Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the Patient Interface Module connection for looseness, damage, contamination, strain, or intermittent behavior when the cable is moved.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Data Cable
steps.instructions: Check the patient data cable for damage, bent pins, loose strain relief, contamination, twisting, or pulling damage, and test with a known-good cable if available.

steps.title: Inspect Lead Wires and Patient Cable Accessories
steps.instructions: Check lead wires and electrode connections for damage, poor fit, contamination, or strain that could affect signal stability.

steps.title: Run or Review PIM Test Results
steps.instructions: Perform or review PIM-related testing according to facility procedure and remove the PIM from use if it fails communication or acquisition checks.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good PIM
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible PIM and repeat rhythm printing to determine whether the original PIM is the likely cause.

steps.title: Check for a Device-Specific Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue follows the accessory or remains with the cardiograph to help identify an accessory-side or device-side fault.

steps.title: Restart and Recheck
steps.instructions: Power down the cardiograph, reseat the PIM and patient data cable, restart the unit, and repeat rhythm printing and acquisition testing.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the issue clears after external checks or accessory replacement, document the corrective action and return the device to service after successful functional testing.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed Printer underflow during rhythm printing. Staff reported interrupted rhythm strip printing and intermittent ECG signal acquisition behavior.
CCR.Cause: Printer underflow condition confirmed during rhythm printing. PIM communication path inspected. Patient data cable and PIM connection checked due to intermittent acquisition communication behavior.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from patient use. Checked AC power, printer paper path, PIM connection, patient data cable, and lead wires. Reseated connections and repeated rhythm print testing. Device passed functional testing and was returned to service, or labeled Out of Service for bench evaluation if unresolved.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printer voltage high
description: Troubleshooting Printer voltage high errors on the Philips PageWriter TC70 that may affect printer power regulation, ECG printing reliability, or device dependability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop use of the affected cardiograph during active patient testing and move ECG acquisition to another verified unit if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the PageWriter TC70, confirm the exact Printer voltage high message, and document when the error appears and whether printing or acquisition is affected.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Clinical Use if the Error Is Active
steps.instructions: If the error is present or returns after restart, remove the cardiograph from service and label it Out of Service.

steps.title: Check the External Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the cardiograph directly into a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet and avoid extension cords, power strips, loose receptacles, or questionable shared outlets.

steps.title: Check Battery-Only and AC-Powered Behavior
steps.instructions: Compare whether the error appears on AC power, battery power, or both, and document the observed behavior without repeated testing if the fault is persistent.

steps.title: Inspect the Printer Area Externally
steps.instructions: Open the printer paper drawer and check for jammed paper, incorrect paper loading, debris, damaged latches, liquid intrusion, burnt smell, heat, or discoloration.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Liquid Exposure
steps.instructions: Inspect the exterior, printer housing, AC inlet, battery area, and casing for cracks, impact damage, residue, staining, loose panels, or evidence of a drop.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph Once
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, disconnect AC briefly, reconnect to a verified AC outlet, power back on, and confirm whether the Printer voltage high error returns.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Printer Function Check if the Error Clears
steps.instructions: If the error clears, perform a controlled print test and confirm normal paper feed, acceptable print quality, and no abnormal noise, smell, heat, or returning printer voltage message.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Deep Internal Troubleshooting in Clinical Areas
steps.instructions: Do not open the cardiograph, bypass printer protections, defeat safety features, or continue using the device with an active printer voltage error.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed Printer voltage high error. Staff reported printer function was unavailable or unreliable during ECG use.
CCR.Cause: Verified Printer voltage high error. External AC outlet, printer drawer, paper path, and visible physical condition were checked. Error persisted after restart, indicating likely internal printer supply voltage or main board fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed cardiograph from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Printer voltage condition could not be cleared through external troubleshooting.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printer voltage low
description: Troubleshooting guide for Philips PageWriter TC70 Printer voltage low errors that may disable ECG printing or indicate a low printer supply voltage condition.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the cardiograph from patient use if printing is unreliable and provide another verified ECG machine before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact Printer voltage low message and note whether it appears at startup, printer initialization, print attempt, or intermittently.

steps.title: Connect to Known-Good AC Power
steps.instructions: Plug the PageWriter TC70 directly into a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet and avoid power strips, extension cords, or battery-only operation during testing.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the battery is installed, charged, and not showing a low battery condition before retesting printer operation.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, wait briefly, and restart while connected to AC power. Watch for the error during bootup and printer initialization.

steps.title: Inspect the Printer Paper Drawer
steps.instructions: Open and close the printer paper drawer fully and confirm it latches securely without obstruction, damage, or contamination.

steps.title: Check Printer Paper Installation
steps.instructions: Verify the correct paper is installed in the proper direction and remove any jammed, damaged, wet, wrinkled, or incorrectly loaded paper.

steps.title: Inspect the Visible Printer Area
steps.instructions: Look for paper debris, torn paper, contamination, broken drawer parts, abnormal resistance, odor, heat, or visible damage.

steps.title: Perform a Test Print
steps.instructions: Attempt a controlled test print or non-patient test ECG and observe whether printing completes or the Printer voltage low error returns.

steps.title: Isolate Printer Load Versus General Device Function
steps.instructions: Confirm whether non-printing functions such as startup, touchscreen operation, patient cable recognition, and ECG acquisition preview still work normally.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Deep Internal Troubleshooting in Clinical Areas
steps.instructions: If the error persists after external checks, remove the device from service for bench evaluation without performing internal disassembly in the clinical area.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 reported Printer voltage low during printer initialization or ECG printing. Staff reported ECG printing was unavailable or unreliable.
CCR.Cause: Verified Printer voltage low error on startup or print attempt. AC power, battery status, printer drawer, paper loading, and visible paper path were checked. Error persisted, indicating likely internal printer supply voltage, printer assembly, cable path, or main board-related fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PageWriter TC70 from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation or Philips service due to persistent Printer voltage low error.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printer Door is Open
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 Printer Door is Open error that may prevent ECG printing or indicate a printer door, paper path, latch, or door detection issue.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the cardiograph is needed for an active patient ECG and printing or acquisition is affected, use another available EKG machine. Do not rely on a cardiograph with an unresolved printer error if printed ECG output is required for clinical care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Philips PageWriter TC70 and confirm the exact Printer Door is Open message. Check whether it appears during startup, printing, after loading paper, or when the printer drawer is touched.

steps.title: Check for an Open or Partially Latched Printer Door
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer paper drawer and door area. Open the printer door completely, then close it firmly until it seats flush with the cardiograph housing. Do not slam the door.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: After closing the printer door, restart the PageWriter TC70 and allow it to complete startup. Confirm whether the Printer Door is Open error returns.

steps.title: Inspect Printer Paper Loading
steps.instructions: Open the printer drawer and confirm the paper is loaded correctly. Check for misloaded paper, bent paper, folded paper, jams, debris, or paper interfering with printer door closure.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction or Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer door, hinge, latch, drawer, and surrounding plastic for cracks, broken parts, loose hinge points, warping, foreign material, sticky residue, or contamination.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Door Detection
steps.instructions: With the door closed, gently press near the printer door area and observe whether the error clears or returns. Do not force the door or apply excessive pressure.

steps.title: Test Printer Function
steps.instructions: If the error clears, perform a printer test or print an ECG from a controlled test workflow according to facility practice. Confirm that the printer feeds paper and produces acceptable output.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Was External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after closing the door, reloading paper, removing an obstruction, or restarting, the issue was likely external. If it persists, suspect a latch, door sensor, printer assembly, or internal connection issue.

steps.title: Return to Service If Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears, printer function is verified, and the device passes normal operational checks, return the PageWriter TC70 to service and document the corrective action.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed Printer Door is Open error and printer function was unavailable during attempted ECG printing.
CCR.Cause: Printer door was found partially open/not fully latched. Paper was checked and no jam or obstruction was found.
CCR.Resolution: Opened and closed printer door, verified paper was loaded correctly, restarted cardiograph, and confirmed printer operation. Device passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Check Printer
description: Troubleshooting Check Printer errors on the Philips PageWriter TC70 that may prevent ECG reports from printing due to printer drawer, paper path, printer controller, or internal printer connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Provide another working ECG machine before troubleshooting if the PageWriter TC70 is needed for active ECG acquisition or timed testing. Do not rely on a cardiograph that cannot print required ECG reports.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the cardiograph and confirm the exact Check Printer message. Observe whether it occurs at startup, when opening or closing the printer drawer, during printing, or intermittently during normal use.

steps.title: Remove the Device from Active Clinical Use if Printing Is Required
steps.instructions: If ECG printing is required for clinical workflow, remove the unit from patient use until printer function is verified. Use another ECG machine if immediate ECG documentation is needed.

steps.title: Check Printer Paper Drawer Position
steps.instructions: Open the printer paper drawer fully, inspect that it moves smoothly, and close it firmly and evenly. Confirm the drawer seats completely and the error clears.

steps.title: Check Paper Loading
steps.instructions: Verify correct ECG paper is installed and sitting properly in the tray. Confirm the paper is not jammed, folded, wrinkled, upside down, or interfering with drawer closure.

steps.title: Inspect for Paper Jam or Debris
steps.instructions: With the drawer open, look for torn paper, labels, dust, adhesive residue, or foreign material around the paper path. Remove only loose, accessible debris without forcing printer components.

steps.title: Restart and Recheck the Error
steps.instructions: Power the PageWriter TC70 off, wait briefly, then power it back on. Confirm whether Check Printer returns at startup or only during printing.

steps.title: Perform a Test Print
steps.instructions: Attempt a controlled ECG or system printout according to normal facility workflow. Confirm paper feeds correctly, print quality is readable, no error returns, and the drawer remains latched.

steps.title: Check External Condition of the Printer Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer drawer, latch area, paper tray, and exterior housing for cracks, impact damage, looseness, or signs the device was dropped. Remove the device from service if damage is found.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after reseating the drawer and the printer works normally, the issue was likely drawer seating or paper path related. If the error returns with the drawer seated, correct paper installed, and no jam present, treat it as a device-related service condition.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 reported Check Printer error. Staff reported ECG printer would not reliably print reports.
CCR.Cause: Printer drawer and paper path checked. Error persisted after drawer reseat and paper inspection, indicating likely internal printer controller or printer cable service condition.
CCR.Resolution: Opened and closed printer drawer, verified paper loading, checked for accessible paper jam or debris, restarted unit, and attempted test print. Error returned. Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Printing is disabled with one battery only, please check the battery
description: Troubleshooting a Philips PageWriter TC70 battery configuration error that disables ECG printing when only one battery is installed, detected, or available.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PageWriter TC70 is needed for active patient testing and printing is unavailable, use another verified ECG machine or an approved alternate ECG documentation workflow. Do not rely on the affected unit for printed ECG output until the battery condition is corrected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the cardiograph and confirm the exact message: Printing is disabled with one battery only, please check the battery. Check whether the issue occurs at startup, when attempting to print, while operating on battery power, or after battery replacement or removal.

steps.title: Translate the Error Into the Real-World Condition
steps.instructions: Understand that the cardiograph detects only one installed or available battery. The TC70 may power on, but printing may be disabled until both batteries are installed and detected.

steps.title: Connect the Cardiograph to AC Power
steps.instructions: Plug the unit directly into a known-good AC outlet, avoid loose outlets or extension cords, and confirm normal AC power behavior before checking batteries.

steps.title: Check That Both Battery Bays Are Populated
steps.instructions: Inspect the battery compartment and verify that two approved batteries are installed. If only one battery is present, install the second compatible battery and test printing after the message clears.

steps.title: Reseat Both Batteries
steps.instructions: Power the unit down if appropriate, remove and reinstall both batteries, and confirm each battery is fully seated and latched. Check for loose fit, partial insertion, debris, bent contacts, or obvious damage.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition and Charge Status
steps.instructions: Allow batteries to charge on AC power if depleted. Review battery status if available and look for batteries that are not recognized, not charging, abnormal, cracked, swollen, or damaged.

steps.title: Swap With Known-Good Batteries if Available
steps.instructions: If the error remains, test with a known-good compatible battery set if facility policy allows. If the error clears, the original battery or set is likely the cause. If it remains, suspect device-side battery detection or interface problems.

steps.title: Check for Printer Function After Battery Correction
steps.instructions: After both batteries are installed and detected, perform a controlled test print. Confirm the error does not return, printing is enabled, paper feeds normally, print quality is acceptable, and no additional warnings appear.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If installing, charging, or reseating the second battery clears the error and printing works normally, document the correction and return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displays error 'Printing is disabled with one battery only, please check the battery.' Staff reports ECG printing is disabled.
CCR.Cause: Only one battery was installed in the cardiograph, preventing battery-supported printing.
CCR.Resolution: Installed second approved battery, verified both batteries were seated, connected unit to AC power, cleared error, and completed successful test print. Device returned to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Low battery warning: printing is disabled. Connect the cardiograph to AC power and recharge the batteries
description: Troubleshooting a Philips PageWriter TC70 low battery warning that disables ECG printing due to depleted, missing, uncharged, or failed batteries, or AC power and charging problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PageWriter TC70 is needed for active patient testing and printing is disabled, do not continue using it as the primary ECG device. Use another available ECG machine or connect the unit to verified AC power before continuing.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the PageWriter TC70 and confirm the exact low battery warning is displayed. Confirm whether printing is actually disabled and when the message appears.

steps.title: Connect the Cardiograph to AC Power
steps.instructions: Plug the PageWriter TC70 directly into a known-good AC outlet. Avoid power strips, loose outlets, switched outlets, or damaged receptacles. Confirm the AC power indicator or charging status appears.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for cuts, bent prongs, loose strain relief, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is fully seated at both the wall outlet and the cardiograph. Try a known-good compatible power cord if needed.

steps.title: Check Battery Presence and Seating
steps.instructions: Inspect the battery compartment according to facility procedure. Confirm the battery or batteries are installed and seated correctly. Look for missing, loose, damaged, swollen, hot, or contaminated battery conditions.

steps.title: Allow the Batteries to Recharge
steps.instructions: Leave the PageWriter TC70 connected to AC power long enough for the batteries to recharge. Confirm the charging indicator or battery status improves, then attempt printing again.

steps.title: Test Printing While on AC Power
steps.instructions: With the unit connected to AC power, attempt a test print or ECG print from a non-patient test workflow. If printing works on AC but not on battery, suspect battery capacity, charge state, or battery connection.

steps.title: Check Battery Runtime
steps.instructions: After charging, disconnect AC power and operate the cardiograph on battery. Observe whether the battery level drops quickly or the low battery warning returns immediately.

steps.title: Replace the Battery if Needed
steps.instructions: Install a known-good or approved replacement battery if the existing battery is dead, missing, unable to charge, or unable to support printing. Reconnect AC power and confirm the device recognizes the replacement battery.

steps.title: Verify Final Operation
steps.instructions: Confirm the PageWriter TC70 powers on normally, recognizes AC power, shows acceptable battery status, prints successfully, and does not display the low battery warning.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed 'Low battery warning: printing is disabled. Connect the cardiograph to AC power and recharge the batteries' and staff reported the unit would not print ECGs.
CCR.Cause: Battery was depleted and the cardiograph had not been charging from AC power. Power cord seating and battery status were checked before testing print operation.
CCR.Resolution: Connected cardiograph to known-good AC power, verified charging status, reseated battery, allowed battery to recharge, and completed successful ECG print test. Unit returned to service after low battery warning cleared.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) communication is incorrectly configured
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 FPGA communication configuration error that may affect ECG acquisition, startup, printing, data transfer, or normal system operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the PageWriter TC70 from patient use if the error appears during startup, ECG acquisition, printing, transmission, or normal operation. Use another verified ECG machine for patient testing.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the cardiograph on in a controlled Clinical Engineering area and confirm the exact FPGA communication configuration error. Document when the error occurs and whether it affects startup, ECG acquisition, printing, or transfer.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the unit to a known-good hospital-grade outlet, avoid questionable power sources, verify AC indication, and charge or test with a known-good battery if battery condition is questionable.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart
steps.instructions: Shut down normally if possible, disconnect AC power, allow the unit to sit briefly, reconnect AC power, and restart. If the error clears, complete functional verification before return to service.

steps.title: Disconnect External Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove nonessential external connections such as network cable, USB devices, barcode scanner, external keyboard or mouse, cart accessories, and external data connections, then restart the cardiograph.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Interface Module and ECG Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the Patient Interface Module, patient data cable, and lead wires for bent pins, damage, contamination, cracked housings, strain damage, or loose connections. Reconnect securely or test with known-good accessories if available.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the exterior, display, keyboard or touchscreen area, printer area, connectors, and battery compartment for drop damage, cracked case, liquid intrusion, corrosion, burn smell, heat discoloration, or loose ports.

steps.title: Run Available User or Biomed-Level Self-Tests
steps.instructions: If the device allows access past the error, run appropriate diagnostics or operational checks for startup, display, touchscreen, ECG acquisition, printer function, network transfer, and logged system errors.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Deep Internal Repair at the Point of Use
steps.instructions: Treat the device as having an internal system fault after external causes are ruled out. Do not continue bedside troubleshooting or rely on the unit for patient ECGs if the error remains.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after external checks, verify successful startup, no recurring error, ECG acquisition, printing, battery and AC operation, Patient Interface Module operation, and required facility checks before returning to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 reported Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) communication is incorrectly configured error and staff reported unit could not be trusted for normal ECG use.
CCR.Cause: External power, battery, accessory, patient cable, and visible damage checks were performed. Error persisted, indicating likely internal FPGA communication, configuration, firmware, or system electronics fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PageWriter TC70 from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for Philips or authorized repair evaluation. Device was not returned to clinical use due to unresolved internal error.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) is not connected
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) not connected error that may affect startup, ECG acquisition, waveform display, printing, or device reliability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the PageWriter TC70 from patient use if the error appears during startup, ECG acquisition, printing, or normal operation. Use another verified ECG machine for patient testing.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the cardiograph on in a controlled Biomed area and confirm the exact Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) is not connected message. Observe when the error appears and check for boot, display, ECG acquisition, PIM, printer, or repeated internal error symptoms.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph Once
steps.instructions: Power the unit down normally if possible, disconnect AC power, remove battery power if applicable and allowed, wait briefly, then reconnect power and restart. If resolved, complete full operational testing before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Check the Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the cardiograph directly into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Avoid power strips, extension cords, loose outlets, or switched receptacles. Verify stable AC power and normal power indicator behavior.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC cord and exterior housing for damage, loose connectors, impact damage, liquid intrusion, missing covers, burning smell, unusual heat, or other signs of contamination or damage.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the battery is properly seated and charging if installed. Check whether the error occurs on AC power only, battery power only, with the battery installed, or after battery removal and reseating if allowed by facility procedure.

steps.title: Disconnect External Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove nonessential external accessories such as USB devices, barcode scanner, network cable, keyboard, mouse, cart accessories, or other peripherals. Restart and reconnect accessories one at a time if the error clears.

steps.title: Check the Patient Interface Module and ECG Cable
steps.instructions: Disconnect and inspect the PIM, ECG cable, and lead set for bent pins, loose connections, cracked housing, fluid contamination, cable strain, or obvious damage. Test with known-good compatible accessories if available.

steps.title: Check Network and Configuration-Related Behavior
steps.instructions: If the error appears during startup or after login, disconnect the network cable and restart the device to determine whether it can boot locally without network communication.

steps.title: Run Available User-Level or Biomed-Level Checks
steps.instructions: If diagnostics or operational checks are accessible, verify startup, touchscreen response, ECG waveform display, PIM communication, printer operation, retained date/time and configuration, and absence of additional internal errors.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Error Is External or Internal
steps.instructions: If the error clears after correcting power, removing an accessory, or replacing a damaged cable, document the external cause and complete functional testing. If the error remains after external checks, treat it as an internal cardiograph fault.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error no longer appears and the device passes full operational testing, acquire a test ECG, verify waveform quality, confirm PIM communication, confirm printer output, clean the unit as needed, and return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 reported Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) is not connected error during startup. Device unavailable for patient ECG acquisition.
CCR.Cause: External power, accessory, PIM, ECG cable, network, and restart checks did not clear the error. Condition indicates likely internal cardiograph electronics or FPGA communication failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for Philips Response Center or service evaluation. Patient testing transferred to alternate ECG machine.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Selected ECG(s), exceeds available Archive space on USB Memory Stick
description: Troubleshooting USB archive space errors when selected ECG records cannot be exported from a Philips PageWriter TC70 due to USB storage, file system, or batch-size limitations.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot USB export or archive functions while the PageWriter TC70 is actively being used for patient testing. If ECG capture is needed, allow clinical staff to complete the ECG first or use another verified ECG machine.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error message is Selected ECG(s), exceeds available Archive space on USB Memory Stick. Determine whether it occurs with one ECG, multiple ECGs, a large batch, a specific USB memory stick, or multiple USB memory sticks.

steps.title: Confirm the ECG Machine Still Functions Normally
steps.instructions: Verify the PageWriter TC70 powers on normally and can acquire ECGs, access stored ECGs, and print if applicable. This helps isolate the issue to USB archiving.

steps.title: Check the USB Memory Stick Capacity
steps.instructions: Remove the USB memory stick and check available space on a computer. Confirm whether the drive is full or nearly full and use a drive with adequate free space.

steps.title: Delete Unwanted Files from the USB Memory Stick
steps.instructions: Delete old ECG archives, duplicate exports, unrelated files, or other stored data from the USB memory stick. Reinsert the USB stick and retry the archive.

steps.title: Reduce the Number of ECGs Selected
steps.instructions: If a large ECG batch was selected, try exporting a smaller group. Archive records in smaller batches if needed.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good USB Memory Stick
steps.instructions: Use a facility-approved USB memory stick with adequate free space. Avoid damaged, old, unknown, encrypted, or unusually formatted drives.

steps.title: Check for USB Connection Problems
steps.instructions: Inspect the USB connector on the memory stick and the ECG machine for bent connectors, contamination, looseness, poor seating, or intermittent detection.

steps.title: Confirm the Error Is Not Caused by File System Limitations
steps.instructions: If the USB stick has free space but export still fails, try a smaller archive or another properly formatted, facility-approved USB memory stick.

steps.title: Verify Final Operation
steps.instructions: Export a small test ECG archive and confirm the exported ECG file appears on the USB stick when checked on a computer. Confirm final device status before returning it to service.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Philips PageWriter TC70 displays Selected ECG(s), exceeds available Archive space on USB Memory Stick when attempting to archive selected ECG records to USB.
CCR.Cause: USB memory stick did not have enough usable archive space, or selected ECG batch exceeded USB file system or storage limitations.
CCR.Resolution: Deleted unwanted files from USB memory stick and retried export. ECG archive completed successfully. Verified ECG machine operation and returned device to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Timed ECG number exceeds available number in Main Archive
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 timed ECG archive error that can prevent additional timed ECG records from being saved to the Main Archive due to storage limits or file buildup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop using the PageWriter TC70 for new patient ECG acquisition if ECGs cannot be saved reliably. Move patient testing to another verified ECG machine before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message is 'Timed ECG number exceeds available number in Main Archive' and determine whether it appears during timed ECG acquisition, saving, archive review, or after multiple ECGs.

steps.title: Check Whether the Main Archive Is Full or Near Capacity
steps.instructions: Access the Main Archive and review stored ECG records. Look for old, duplicate, test, training, or unwanted ECG files that may be using available archive capacity.

steps.title: Delete Unwanted Files From the Main Archive
steps.instructions: Delete unneeded ECG files according to facility policy. Do not delete patient ECGs unless they are confirmed duplicates, test records, unwanted files, or already stored in the approved clinical record system.

steps.title: Verify Records Are Properly Transmitted or Backed Up Before Deletion
steps.instructions: Confirm records have been transmitted to the ECG management system, network archive, EMR, or other approved location before clearing patient ECGs.

steps.title: Restart the PageWriter TC70
steps.instructions: Restart the cardiograph after archive cleanup and confirm the unit boots normally without storage or archive-related warnings.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Timed ECG Save Test
steps.instructions: In a Biomed or approved test setting, perform a test timed ECG workflow if allowed and verify the record saves successfully to the Main Archive.

steps.title: Check for Related Storage or Workflow Issues
steps.instructions: If the error returns quickly, check for excessive duplicate ECGs, test ECGs, repeated timed ECG records, failed transmission workflow, or poor archive maintenance.

steps.title: Inspect Basic External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm stable AC power or charged battery operation during archive review and deletion. Check for abnormal shutdowns, frozen screens, failed touch response, or repeated restarts.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the device to service only after unwanted files are deleted, the unit restarts normally, and a timed ECG saves successfully.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displays error 'Timed ECG number exceeds available number in Main Archive' while saving a timed ECG. Staff reported timed ECGs could not be saved.
CCR.Cause: Main Archive was full or near capacity due to stored ECG files, preventing additional timed ECG records from being saved.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from active use during evaluation. Reviewed Main Archive, confirmed stored ECG file buildup, deleted unwanted files per facility policy, restarted unit, and verified timed ECG save function passed. Unit returned to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Archive function could not be completed
description: Troubleshooting Philips PageWriter TC70 archive errors that prevent ECG records from being saved, transferred, or stored properly.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Move patient ECG testing to another verified ECG machine if needed. Do not rely on the affected cardiograph if ECG storage, archive, or transfer cannot be confirmed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message Archive function could not be completed. Please restart cardiograph and try again. Determine when the error appears and whether it is repeatable.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: Power the PageWriter TC70 off normally, wait briefly, power it back on, and attempt the same archive function again.

steps.title: Confirm the ECG Record Is Still Available
steps.instructions: Check whether the ECG record can still be viewed, printed, saved, resent, or archived after restart.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Verify stable AC power, inspect the power connection, and confirm the unit is not losing power or behaving erratically during archive attempts.

steps.title: Check Network or Destination Availability
steps.instructions: If archive depends on network transfer, verify network connection, location or profile settings, destination system availability, and whether other cardiographs transmit normally.

steps.title: Check External Connection Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect and reseat communication cables, network cables, docking connections, or external storage media used by the facility workflow.

steps.title: Confirm Correct User Workflow
steps.instructions: Review the user steps before the error appeared and confirm the ECG was finalized, accepted, saved, or routed correctly before archive was attempted.

steps.title: Check Storage or Archive Queue Behavior
steps.instructions: Review whether multiple ECGs are pending archive or transfer and whether stored ECGs can be accessed normally.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Acquire a test ECG or simulator ECG and confirm the unit can power on, acquire, display, print if required, save, and archive or transmit without error.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If restart restores archive function, the ECG record is available, and the unit passes checks, document the finding and return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed error: Archive function could not be completed. User reported ECG archive would not complete.
CCR.Cause: Archive error was confirmed. Cardiograph required restart before archive function could complete. No external power, workflow, or connection issue was found during basic checks.
CCR.Resolution: Restarted cardiograph and repeated archive process. Test ECG was saved and archived successfully. Unit passed basic functional check and was returned to service.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: Archive process was terminated due to an error Archive error
description: Troubleshooting a Philips PageWriter TC70 archive process error that may prevent ECG storage, export, transfer, or completion of the archive workflow.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PageWriter TC70 is being used for an active ECG, complete patient care using another verified ECG machine if needed. Do not continue relying on the cardiograph if ECG storage, archive completion, or transfer is uncertain.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed error is Archive process was terminated due to an error. Determine whether the error occurs during automatic ECG archive, manual save, network transfer, USB export, startup, shutdown, or intermittent use.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph
steps.instructions: Power the PageWriter TC70 off normally, wait briefly, power it back on, and allow startup to complete. Attempt the same archive action again and verify whether the error clears.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the cardiograph directly to a known-good AC outlet. Verify stable AC power and confirm the unit is not operating from a depleted or unstable battery during archive attempts.

steps.title: Check Available Storage and Archive Behavior
steps.instructions: Review whether the internal archive appears full, slow to respond, or unable to display stored ECGs. Delete or export old ECGs only according to facility policy, retention requirements, and clinical workflow.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Workflow
steps.instructions: Ask the user what action was being performed when the error occurred. Confirm whether they were saving locally, sending to a network destination, exporting to USB, or repeating the same failed archive action.

steps.title: Check Network Connection if Archiving to a Remote System
steps.instructions: If archiving to a server, MUSE system, shared destination, or network location, inspect Ethernet connection, wall jack, cable condition, network status, and whether other ECG carts are transferring normally.

steps.title: Check External Media if Exporting
steps.instructions: If the error occurs during USB or removable media export, inspect the media, confirm it is not write-protected or full, and try a known-good approved USB drive if facility policy allows.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, and Patient Data Fields
steps.instructions: Verify the cardiograph date and time are correct and confirm required patient demographic fields are entered if the archive workflow requires them.

steps.title: Test With a New Sample ECG in a Controlled Setting
steps.instructions: Create a test ECG according to facility policy or use an appropriate simulator/test workflow. Attempt to save, print, and archive the test record to determine whether the issue is record-specific or device-wide.

steps.title: Narrow Down the Failure
steps.instructions: Determine whether the failure is tied to network transfer, USB export, local save, workflow, external connection, or the cardiograph itself.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If restart or correction of an external cause resolves the error, verify ECG acquisition, printing, local save, archive or transfer completion, and no repeat error before returning the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed Archive process was terminated due to an error during ECG archive attempt. User reported ECG could not complete archive process.
CCR.Cause: Archive error confirmed. Restart attempted. External checks performed for AC power, archive workflow, network or USB media connection, record handling, and sample ECG archive test.
CCR.Resolution: Restarted cardiograph and retested archive function. Verified ECG acquisition, printing, local save, and archive completion. If unresolved, removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation or repair.

title: Philips PageWriter TC70 Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Error: An unknown error occurred when accessing the storage capacity of the selected archive
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips PageWriter TC70 archive storage capacity error that may prevent ECG storage, retrieval, export, or archive access.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on the affected archive function for active ECG testing. Use another verified ECG machine if ECG capture, storage, or transfer is required for patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the PageWriter TC70, repeat the archive action, and confirm the exact error message and when it occurs.

steps.title: Identify the Selected Archive Location
steps.instructions: Determine whether the selected archive is internal storage, USB/removable media, network storage, a shared folder, or an unavailable configured destination.

steps.title: Check for Removable Media Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect USB or removable media for damage, full storage, write protection, unsupported format, corruption, or loose connection. Try known-good approved media if allowed.

steps.title: Check Internal Storage Availability
steps.instructions: If using the internal archive, check whether ECGs can be saved, retrieved, and accessed normally or whether repeated storage errors occur.

steps.title: Restart the Cardiograph and Recheck
steps.instructions: Power the PageWriter TC70 off normally, restart it, and repeat the archive action to determine whether the error clears.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the cardiograph to a known-good AC outlet and confirm normal startup without low battery, shutdown, or abnormal power behavior.

steps.title: Verify Network Archive Availability
steps.instructions: If the archive is network-based, check Ethernet or wireless connection, network profile, server availability, IP configuration, recent network changes, and whether other ECG machines can access the same archive.

steps.title: Check Archive Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct archive is selected, the destination still exists, credentials have not changed, and the folder path or server address is correct.

steps.title: Test Basic ECG Functions
steps.instructions: Verify the device can power on, acquire an ECG, print, display patient data, save, retrieve, and transfer or export ECGs according to facility workflow.

steps.title: Isolate the Failure
steps.instructions: Determine whether the problem follows one USB device, one network archive, or all archive locations. Treat device-wide archive failure as unreliable.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the PageWriter TC70 to service only after archive function and ECG save, retrieve, print, and transfer workflow are verified.

CCR.Complaint: Philips PageWriter TC70 displayed “An unknown error occurred when accessing the storage capacity of the selected archive” when attempting to access the selected ECG archive.
CCR.Cause: Selected archive storage capacity could not be verified. External media, network archive access, power, or archive configuration may be involved. If unresolved, internal storage, software, or system-level failure may be present.
CCR.Resolution: Verified reported error, checked selected archive location, inspected applicable media and network access, restarted device, and tested ECG save/retrieve/print workflow. Device was returned to service only if archive function passed verification; otherwise it was removed from service and escalated for Philips support review.

title: Philips IntelliVue 6000 Series Patient Monitor - ECG Lead-Off / Waveform Artifact
description: Troubleshooting ECG lead-off, noisy waveform, intermittent ECG dropout, artifact, or poor electrode/contact issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable and being monitored appropriately before touching ECG leads, cables, modules, or monitor connections. If the ECG waveform is unreliable during active monitoring, have clinical staff move the patient to another working monitor or use an alternate monitoring method. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained while the suspect ECG issue is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact ECG Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: ECG lead-off message; One lead not displaying; All ECG waveforms missing; Intermittent waveform dropout; Excessive artifact or baseline wander; False heart rate; ECG works only when the cable is held in a certain position; Problem follows one patient, one room, or one monitor. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to electrodes, patient cable, leadwire set, ECG trunk cable, parameter input, or monitor-side issue.

steps.title: Check Whether the Patient Is Actively Connected Correctly
steps.instructions: Verify with clinical staff that the electrodes are placed correctly, firmly attached, and not dried out, loose, expired, or applied over heavy hair, lotion, sweat, or poor skin prep. Expected outcome: ECG electrodes have good patient contact and are not the obvious cause of lead-off or artifact.

steps.title: Replace the ECG Electrodes
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff apply fresh electrodes and reattach the leadwires. Expected outcome: If the lead-off or artifact clears after electrode replacement, document the issue as electrode/contact related and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Leadwire Set
steps.instructions: Check each snap/clip connection for looseness, corrosion, bent contacts, broken insulation, dried fluid, or intermittent connection when moved gently. Expected outcome: Leadwires are physically intact and maintain a stable connection.

steps.title: Swap the Leadwire Set With a Known-Good Set
steps.instructions: Use a compatible known-good ECG leadwire set approved for the monitor configuration. Expected outcome: If the ECG waveform stabilizes or the lead-off message clears, the original leadwire set is defective. Remove it from service and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Trunk Cable
steps.instructions: Check the trunk cable for cuts, crushed areas, stretched strain reliefs, fluid contamination, damaged pins, loose connector fit, or intermittent response when the cable is moved. Expected outcome: The ECG trunk cable is not visibly damaged and does not cause dropout when handled.

steps.title: Swap the ECG Trunk Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the ECG trunk cable with a known-good compatible cable. Expected outcome: If the problem clears, the original trunk cable is faulty. Remove the cable from service and stop.

steps.title: Check the ECG Input Connection at the Monitor or Parameter Module
steps.instructions: Confirm the ECG cable is fully seated in the correct ECG input. Look for bent pins, cracked connector housing, contamination, or looseness at the input jack. Expected outcome: The ECG connection is secure and the input port does not appear damaged.

steps.title: Check for Module or Parameter Recognition Issues
steps.instructions: If the IntelliVue 6000 Series configuration uses a parameter module or external acquisition device, confirm the module is properly seated, recognized by the monitor, and not showing a related parameter failure. Expected outcome: The ECG parameter is available and recognized by the monitor.

steps.title: Reseat the Parameter Module or Acquisition Connection if Safe
steps.instructions: Only when the patient is no longer dependent on that monitor, remove and reseat the ECG-related module or acquisition connection. Expected outcome: If ECG returns after reseating, document the connection issue and continue monitoring for recurrence.

steps.title: Compare With Another Known-Good Monitor or Room Setup
steps.instructions: Move the same known-good ECG cable set to another compatible monitor, or test the suspect monitor with a known-good cable set and simulator if available. Expected outcome: The issue is isolated to the patient/electrodes, cable set, module, monitor input, or room environment.

steps.title: Use an ECG Simulator if Available
steps.instructions: Connect a compatible ECG simulator using known-good cables to verify whether the monitor displays a stable ECG waveform and accurate heart rate. Expected outcome: A stable simulator waveform suggests the monitor is functioning and the issue is likely patient contact, electrodes, or cables.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Motion Artifact
steps.instructions: Ask whether artifact occurs during patient movement, bed movement, shivering, poor electrode adhesion, transport, or use of nearby high-noise equipment. Expected outcome: Artifact source is identified as movement, poor contact, or environmental interference rather than monitor failure.

steps.title: Review Alarm and Waveform Behavior
steps.instructions: Note whether the monitor shows lead-off, asystole, noisy ECG, intermittent heart rate, or missing waveform. Confirm whether SpO2 pulse rate remains stable while ECG is unstable. Expected outcome: The failure pattern supports either ECG acquisition trouble or a broader monitor/module issue.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If external accessories check good and the ECG parameter remains abnormal, restart the monitor only after clinical staff move the patient to alternate monitoring. Expected outcome: If ECG returns normally after restart, document the event and monitor for recurrence. If it returns repeatedly, escalate.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: With known-good electrodes/cables or an ECG simulator, confirm ECG waveform stability, heart rate display, lead selection, and alarm response. Expected outcome: The monitor displays a stable ECG waveform without lead-off messages or excessive artifact.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported ECG lead-off messages and intermittent waveform artifact on a Philips IntelliVue 6000 Series patient monitor.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found a damaged ECG leadwire set causing intermittent lead-off when the cable was moved.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the ECG leadwire set with a known-good compatible set, verified stable ECG waveform and heart rate display, and removed the damaged leadwire set from service.

helpfulDetails: Patient moved to alternate monitor before troubleshooting
Skin contact or electrode condition noted
ECG trunk cable inspected and swapped
Lead-off message or artifact behavior observed
Whether issue followed cable, patient, room, module, or monitor
Accessories removed from service, if applicable

title: Philips IntelliVue 6000 Series Patient Monitor - SPO2 SIGNAL DROPOUT OR SENSOR RECOGNITION FAILURE
description: Troubleshooting SpO2 dropout, poor signal, intermittent readings, or sensor recognition failures before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable before disconnecting or swapping SpO2 sensors, cables, or modules. If continuous SpO2 monitoring is required, move the patient to another working monitor or parameter source first. Expected outcome: Patient oxygen saturation monitoring is maintained without relying on a questionable SpO2 channel.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact SpO2 Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: No SpO2 value displayed; SpO2 drops out intermittently; Sensor not recognized; Poor signal or low perfusion message; Pleth waveform missing or unstable; Readings differ from another monitor; Issue follows one patient, one sensor, one cable, or one monitor. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to sensor, patient condition, cable, parameter module, monitor configuration, or internal hardware.

steps.title: Verify the Device Is Not Being Used on an Active Patient Before Testing
steps.instructions: Do not perform repeated disconnects, cable manipulation, or reboot testing while the device is actively relied on for patient monitoring. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed only when safe.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Application Site
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor is placed correctly and that the patient has adequate perfusion at the measurement site. Check for cold extremities, motion, nail polish, artificial nails, poor circulation, edema, or incorrect sensor placement. Expected outcome: SpO2 signal improves when the sensor site is appropriate and properly applied. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Check the sensor for cracked housing, damaged cable jacket, exposed wires, contamination, bent connector pins, loose adhesive, or improper patient size. Expected outcome: Sensor is physically intact, clean, compatible, and appropriate for the patient. If the sensor is damaged or incorrect, replace it and stop if readings return.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Sensor
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good Philips-compatible SpO2 sensor of the correct type. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the sensor and displays a stable pleth waveform and SpO2 value. If the known-good sensor works, remove the original sensor from use.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Extension Cable or Adapter Cable
steps.instructions: Check for loose connections, damaged strain relief, bent pins, worn locking tabs, intermittent response when lightly moved, or contamination in the connector. Expected outcome: Cable connection remains secure and signal does not drop out during gentle movement. If the issue follows the cable, replace the cable and stop.

steps.title: Reconnect the Sensor and Cable Firmly
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the SpO2 sensor, extension cable, and monitor-side connector. Make sure each connector is fully seated. Expected outcome: Sensor recognition returns and the SpO2 parameter remains stable. If this resolves the issue, document a loose connection.

steps.title: Check for Port or Module-Specific Behavior
steps.instructions: If the IntelliVue 6000 Series configuration uses a parameter module, MMS, or external module rack, confirm the SpO2 source is properly seated and recognized by the monitor. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the parameter source and displays SpO2 without intermittent loss. If reseating the module restores SpO2, monitor the device briefly before returning it to service.

steps.title: Swap the Parameter Source When Available
steps.instructions: If a compatible known-good parameter module, MMS, or SpO2-capable source is available, test the monitor with that known-good source. Expected outcome: The failure either follows the original module/source or remains with the monitor/connection point. If the problem follows the module, remove that module from service.

steps.title: Check Monitor Configuration and Parameter Display
steps.instructions: Confirm SpO2 is enabled, assigned to the expected parameter area, and not hidden due to profile, screen layout, or parameter priority settings. Expected outcome: SpO2 appears in the expected location when the parameter source is connected and active. If configuration was the issue, restore the correct monitoring profile or screen layout.

steps.title: Check for Alarm or INOP Messages
steps.instructions: Review the monitor for SpO2-related messages such as no sensor, sensor off, poor signal, low perfusion, or parameter unavailable. Expected outcome: The displayed message matches the observed failure and helps separate patient/sensor problems from device recognition problems.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not in active clinical use, power cycle the monitor or reinitialize the parameter source according to local Clinical Engineering practice. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication recognition issues clear after restart. If the issue returns, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental or Interference Causes
steps.instructions: Check whether dropout occurs during patient movement, cable tension, electrosurgical activity, transport, poor cable routing, or when the sensor cable is stretched or pinched. Expected outcome: Dropout is not caused by motion, strain, routing, or external interference. If rerouting or strain relief resolves the issue, document the correction.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor if Needed
steps.instructions: Place the same known-good sensor and cable on another compatible monitor or test the suspect monitor with another known-good setup. Expected outcome: The fault is isolated to the sensor, cable, module, monitor, or patient/application condition.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: With a known-good sensor, cable, and parameter source, verify that the monitor recognizes SpO2, displays a stable pleth waveform, shows a believable saturation value, and responds appropriately to sensor removal. Expected outcome: SpO2 acquisition is stable and sensor recognition is reliable.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue 6000 Series monitor intermittently lost SpO2 readings and displayed sensor recognition issues.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the SpO2 extension cable had an intermittent connection at the sensor interface, causing signal dropout when the cable was moved.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the SpO2 extension cable, verified stable pleth waveform and SpO2 reading with a known-good sensor, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient was moved to another monitor before troubleshooting
SpO2 sensor type and condition checked
Any SpO2 INOP or alarm message observed
Whether dropout occurred with motion or cable movement
Whether device was returned to service or sent for repair

title: Philips IntelliVue 6000 Series Patient Monitor - NIBP Measurement Failure / Cuff Timeout
description: Troubleshooting NIBP cuff timeout, failed blood pressure readings, inflation problems, leaks, hose issues, and patient-related measurement interruptions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable before troubleshooting the NIBP function. Do not repeatedly cycle the cuff on an active patient if readings are failing. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed by an unreliable NIBP measurement.

steps.title: Use an Alternate Method if Needed
steps.instructions: If blood pressure is clinically needed, have staff use another monitor, manual cuff, or approved backup method before continuing troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Blood pressure monitoring continues safely while the IntelliVue monitor is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what occurred during the NIBP attempt. Determine whether the issue is: Cuff does not inflate; Cuff inflates but times out; Cuff inflates and deflates repeatedly; Reading fails only on one patient; Reading fails on all patients; NIBP alarm, INOP, or timeout message appears; Measurement stops before displaying systolic, diastolic, or MAP values. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to cuff, hose, patient condition, settings, or monitor NIBP hardware.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Application Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the cuff is not being used on an arm with IV lines, injury, restricted circulation, or contraindications. Verify the patient is still, supported, and not moving during measurement. Expected outcome: Motion, poor placement, and unsafe cuff application are ruled out.

steps.title: Verify Correct Cuff Size
steps.instructions: Inspect the cuff size against the patient’s limb circumference. A cuff that is too small, too large, loose, or poorly wrapped can cause timeout or inaccurate readings. Expected outcome: The cuff is appropriately sized and positioned.

steps.title: Inspect Cuff Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the cuff marker is aligned correctly over the artery and the cuff is wrapped snugly without folds, gaps, or twisting. Expected outcome: The cuff is applied in a way that supports a valid NIBP measurement.

steps.title: Inspect the Cuff for Damage
