steps.instructions: Inspect the cuff size, limb placement, artery marker alignment, and snugness. A cuff that is too loose, too small, too large, or incorrectly positioned can cause long inflation cycles or timeout. Expected outcome: The cuff is correctly sized and applied before repeating the NIBP measurement. If proper cuff placement resolves the issue, return the monitor to use and document the finding.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Cuff for Damage
steps.instructions: Look for torn material, damaged bladder, stretched Velcro, loose fittings, or air leakage from the cuff. Expected outcome: The cuff is physically intact and able to hold pressure. If the cuff is damaged, replace it with a known-good compatible cuff and retest.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Hose
steps.instructions: Check the hose for kinks, cracks, loose connectors, pinched sections, contamination, or damaged fittings at both the cuff and monitor end. Expected outcome: The hose is open, secure, and free of visible damage. If the hose is damaged or questionable, replace it with a known-good compatible hose and repeat the measurement.

steps.title: Check the Hose-to-Monitor Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the NIBP hose is fully seated in the IntelliVue X2 NIBP connector. A partially connected hose may allow inflation but prevent stable pressure measurement. Expected outcome: The hose connection is secure and the monitor can attempt a normal measurement.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Cuff and Hose
steps.instructions: Remove the original cuff and hose. Connect a known-good Philips-compatible cuff and hose set. Perform an NIBP test on an appropriate simulator or approved test setup. Expected outcome: If the monitor completes the measurement with known-good accessories, the issue was likely accessory-related. Replace the defective cuff or hose and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check NIBP Settings and Mode
steps.instructions: Verify the NIBP measurement mode, patient category, interval settings, and alarm configuration are appropriate for the clinical area and patient type. Expected outcome: The NIBP setup matches the intended use and is not being affected by an incorrect mode or patient category.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor When Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is off the patient and no active monitoring is affected, power cycle the IntelliVue X2 and repeat the NIBP test using known-good accessories. Expected outcome: A temporary software or module state clears and the NIBP measurement completes normally. If the issue resolves, perform a final functional check before returning the monitor to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Timeout Across Accessories
steps.instructions: Repeat the NIBP test using known-good accessories. Note whether the pump inflates normally, inflates slowly, stops early, or continues until timeout. Expected outcome: If NIBP timeout continues with known-good cuff and hose, external causes have likely been ruled out.

steps.title: Inspect for Abnormal Pump Behavior
steps.instructions: Listen for unusual pump noise, weak inflation, repeated cycling, air leakage, or failure to deflate properly. Do not open the monitor or perform internal repair during floor troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Abnormal pump or valve behavior supports removal from service for bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue X2 repeatedly timed out during NIBP measurement attempts.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the original NIBP hose connection was loose at the monitor, preventing the cuff pressure from stabilizing during measurement.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the NIBP hose, tested the monitor with a known-good cuff and hose, verified successful NIBP measurement, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient monitor removed from active use before troubleshooting
NIBP timeout message or behavior observed
Patient movement or clinical factors reported
Hose connection verified at monitor and cuff
Environmental factors present during the issue
Indicator lights, alarms, or displayed messages observed
Final device status: returned to service or sent for repair

title: Philips IntelliVue X2 Patient Monitor - SpO2 Sensor Or Acquisition Failure
description: Troubleshooting IntelliVue X2 SpO2 sensor, cable, connector, patient signal, and acquisition problems before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips IntelliVue X2 is not the only active source of oxygen saturation monitoring for a patient before disconnecting sensors or accessories. If SpO2 monitoring is clinically required, have staff move the patient to another working monitor or use an alternate approved monitoring method first. Expected outcome: Patient oxygenation monitoring is maintained while troubleshooting is performed safely.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact SpO2 Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: No SpO2 value displayed; Dashed SpO2 reading; SpO2 sensor not detected; Poor signal or low perfusion message; Intermittent SpO2 dropout; Inaccurate or unstable readings; Pleth waveform absent or poor quality; Failure follows the sensor, cable, patient, or X2 module. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient condition, sensor, cable, connector, configuration, or internal acquisition failure.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Sensor Site
steps.instructions: Verify that the sensor is applied to an appropriate site and is not loose, misaligned, too tight, wet, contaminated, or placed over nail polish, artificial nails, poor perfusion, excessive motion, or bright ambient light. Expected outcome: A proper sensor site produces a stable SpO2 value and pleth waveform. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Remove the sensor from service temporarily and inspect it for cracked plastic, damaged LEDs, torn wrap, exposed wiring, fluid intrusion, contamination, or bent contacts. Expected outcome: The sensor is physically intact and appropriate for the patient type. If the sensor is damaged, replace it and stop once normal SpO2 acquisition is confirmed.

steps.title: Confirm Sensor Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify the sensor type is compatible with the IntelliVue X2 SpO2 technology and the connected SpO2 cable. Avoid mixing incompatible Philips, Masimo, Nellcor, or third-party accessories unless approved for that monitor configuration. Expected outcome: The sensor, adapter cable, and monitor configuration match.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor to the same X2 and test on a simulator or appropriate test source. Expected outcome: If SpO2 returns with the known-good sensor, the original sensor is the likely cause. If this resolves the issue, replace the failed sensor and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Extension or Adapter Cable
steps.instructions: Check the SpO2 cable for cuts, kinks, crushed sections, loose strain relief, bent pins, corrosion, contamination, or intermittent response when gently flexed. Expected outcome: Cable movement does not cause SpO2 dropout or sensor detection failure.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the SpO2 extension or adapter cable with a known-good compatible cable. Expected outcome: If the failure clears, the original cable is defective. If this resolves the issue, remove the bad cable from service and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the X2 SpO2 Connector Port
steps.instructions: Look at the SpO2 input port on the IntelliVue X2 for bent pins, cracked housing, debris, dried fluid, looseness, corrosion, or evidence of impact damage. Do not force a connector into a damaged port. Expected outcome: The connector seats securely without looseness or visible damage.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Connection
steps.instructions: With a known-good sensor and cable connected, gently move the cable near the connector while watching the SpO2 value, pleth waveform, and alarm behavior. Expected outcome: The SpO2 signal remains stable during gentle cable movement. If the signal drops out at the X2 connector, suspect port or internal acquisition hardware damage.

steps.title: Check Monitor Display and Parameter Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm SpO2 is enabled on the monitor screen and not hidden by display layout, patient profile, or parameter configuration. Verify that the monitor is not showing a disabled parameter instead of a true acquisition failure. Expected outcome: SpO2 is active, visible, and configured for monitoring.

steps.title: Power Cycle the X2 When Safe
steps.instructions: If the issue appears software-related or the parameter is frozen, power cycle the X2 only after confirming it is not actively needed for patient monitoring. Expected outcome: The SpO2 parameter initializes normally after restart. If this resolves the issue, monitor for recurrence and document the reset.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor or X2
steps.instructions: Test the same known-good sensor and cable on another compatible Philips monitor or X2. Then test the suspect X2 with known-good accessories. Expected outcome: If accessories work elsewhere but fail on the suspect X2, the issue follows the monitor.

steps.title: Check for Related Alarms or INOPs
steps.instructions: Review any displayed SpO2-related messages, technical alarms, or INOPs. Note whether the message indicates sensor off, no sensor, poor signal, equipment malfunction, or no pulse. Expected outcome: The displayed message supports whether the problem is patient/sensor related or monitor hardware related.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Using a compatible SpO2 simulator or approved test method, verify that the IntelliVue X2 can detect the sensor, display SpO2, show a pleth waveform, and alarm appropriately when signal is removed. Expected outcome: The X2 consistently acquires and displays SpO2.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue X2 would not display an SpO2 value and showed intermittent sensor detection during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the SpO2 signal failed with multiple known-good compatible sensors and cables, and the failure followed the X2 monitor.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the IntelliVue X2 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation of the SpO2 input and acquisition hardware.

helpfulDetails: Patient was moved to alternate monitoring before troubleshooting
SpO2 cable or adapter cable swapped
Whether issue followed the sensor, cable, or X2

title: Philips IntelliVue MX100 Patient Monitor - Battery Runtime / Charging Failure
description: Troubleshooting MX100 battery runtime, charging, docking, AC power, battery alarm, and shutdown complaints before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips IntelliVue MX100 is not the only active monitor being relied on for patient surveillance before disconnecting power, removing the monitor, or testing battery operation. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains continuous and safe during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Move the Patient to Another Monitor if Needed
steps.instructions: If the patient requires continued monitoring, have clinical staff transfer monitoring to another verified monitor or approved alternate method before testing the suspect MX100. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not interrupt patient care.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Battery Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: Short battery runtime; Battery not charging; Battery icon not showing charge; Monitor shuts down when removed from power; Low battery alarm occurs shortly after charging; Charging works only in certain docks or locations; Battery percentage changes erratically. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to battery capacity, charging source, dock contact, AC power, or internal power circuitry.

steps.title: Check the Current Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify whether the MX100 is connected to an approved power supply, dock, host monitor, or charging location. Confirm the device shows evidence of external power or charging. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes external power and begins charging if the battery is low. If charging begins normally, allow adequate charge time and stop if the complaint is resolved.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Outlet or Power Strip
steps.instructions: Plug a known-working device or outlet tester into the same receptacle. Avoid relying on an unverified power strip or loose wall outlet. Expected outcome: The outlet provides reliable power. If the outlet fails, report the facility power issue and do not replace monitor parts.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Supply and Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the AC adapter, line cord, strain relief, plug, and connector for damage, looseness, bent pins, fraying, overheating, or signs of liquid contamination. Expected outcome: Power accessories are intact and fit securely. If a damaged cord or adapter is found, remove it from service and retest with a known-good approved power accessory.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Power Accessory or Charging Location
steps.instructions: Connect the MX100 to a verified compatible power source, dock, or charger known to charge another MX100 correctly. Expected outcome: The monitor charges normally with the known-good setup. If the issue follows the original charger or dock, remove that accessory from service.

steps.title: Inspect the Charging Contacts or Docking Area
steps.instructions: Look for debris, bent contacts, cracked plastic, poor seating, or anything preventing full contact between the monitor and the charging interface. Do not scrape or force contacts. Expected outcome: The MX100 seats fully and maintains charging without interruption. If reseating resolves the issue, document the poor connection and return the device only after verifying stable charging.

steps.title: Check for Loose Fit or Intermittent Charging
steps.instructions: Gently move the monitor, cable, or dock connection while observing the charge indicator. Do not stress the connector. Expected outcome: Charging remains stable without flickering, disconnect messages, or power loss. If charging drops in and out, suspect a worn connector, dock, charger, or internal charging path.

steps.title: Review Battery Status Indicators and Alarms
steps.instructions: Note the displayed battery level, charging symbol, battery alarm messages, and whether the percentage increases while connected to power. Expected outcome: Battery status behaves consistently and charge level increases over time. If the level stays fixed, drops while connected, or alarms persist, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Allow a Controlled Charging Period
steps.instructions: Leave the MX100 connected to a verified power source long enough to confirm whether the battery accepts charge. Avoid judging battery condition after only a brief connection. Expected outcome: Battery percentage increases and low battery alarms clear. If the battery does not charge after using verified power, escalation is appropriate.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Runtime Check When Safe
steps.instructions: After charging, remove external power only when the device is not connected to an active patient. Observe whether the monitor remains powered and whether the battery drops unusually fast. Expected outcome: The monitor runs on battery without immediate shutdown or rapid battery depletion. If the monitor shuts off quickly or runtime is unacceptably short, suspect battery deterioration or internal power management failure.

steps.title: Compare With Another MX100 if Available
steps.instructions: Test the same charger, dock, and location with a known-good MX100, or test the suspect MX100 in a known-good charging setup. Expected outcome: The failed component is isolated to the monitor, charger, dock, or location. If the problem stays with the MX100, remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Heat, Odor, Swelling, or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor exterior and battery area for unusual heat, odor, swelling, cracking, or liquid intrusion. Expected outcome: No signs of unsafe battery or power condition are present. If heat, odor, swelling, or damage is found, stop troubleshooting, remove from service, and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Power Cycling
steps.instructions: Avoid repeatedly restarting or draining the monitor in an attempt to force charging. This can mask the complaint and may worsen an unstable power issue. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains controlled and safe.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue MX100 would only run for a few minutes on battery and generated a low battery alarm after being removed from the charger.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall power and charger were functional, charging contacts were clean, and the failure remained with the MX100 during testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed MX100 from service, labeled Out of Service, documented short battery runtime, and sent unit for bench evaluation and battery/power system repair.

helpfulDetails: Battery icon or charging symbol observed
Battery percentage before and after charging
Whether monitor shuts down immediately when disconnected
Any loose connector, intermittent charging, or dock issue
Any unusual heat, odor, swelling, or visible damage

title: Philips IntelliVue MX100 Patient Monitor - Docking Station Or AC Power Connection Issue
description: Troubleshooting MX100 docking, AC power, charging, seating, cable, and external power connection issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the Philips IntelliVue MX100 is not the only active monitor being used for patient surveillance before removing it from a dock, disconnecting AC power, or power cycling the device.

Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained using another verified monitor or approved monitoring method.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Power or Docking Complaint
steps.instructions:
Ask clinical staff what was observed. Determine whether the issue is:

Monitor not charging while docked
Monitor not powering from AC
Battery drains while connected to dock
Intermittent power when the device is moved
No charging icon or external power indicator
Dock does not recognize the monitor
Monitor works on battery but not dock power
Issue follows one dock, one room, one cable, or one MX100

Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to the monitor, dock, AC adapter, wall power, cable, or seating issue.

steps.title: Check for Immediate Safety Concerns
steps.instructions:
Inspect for damaged power cords, cracked adapters, bent connector pins, exposed conductors, burning smell, unusual heat, liquid intrusion, or visible damage to the docking station.

Expected outcome: Any unsafe equipment is removed from service immediately without further use.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Outlet Has Power
steps.instructions:
Plug a known-working device or outlet tester into the same receptacle being used by the docking station or power supply.

Expected outcome: The outlet confirms proper power. If the outlet is dead or unreliable, stop troubleshooting the monitor and report the facility power issue.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Cord Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the AC cord is fully seated at the wall outlet, power brick, and docking station or charger connection point. Look for loose strain relief, partially inserted plugs, or cords pulled tight behind the wall rail or cart.

Expected outcome: Secure power connections restore charging or external power. If resolved, document and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Supply or Adapter
steps.instructions:
Verify the correct Philips-approved power supply is being used for the MX100 docking setup. Check for indicator lights on the power supply if present.

Expected outcome: The correct power supply is connected and shows normal power status. If the wrong or damaged supply is found, replace it with an approved working unit and retest.

steps.title: Reseat the MX100 in the Docking Station
steps.instructions:
Remove the MX100 from the dock, inspect for debris or obstruction, then reseat it fully and evenly. Confirm it locks or sits properly according to the dock design.

Expected outcome: Proper seating restores dock power, charging, or communication. If resolved, document dock seating as the cause and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Docking Contacts and Alignment
steps.instructions:
Look for bent contacts, damaged guides, broken latch points, loose mounting hardware, contamination, dried fluid, or foreign material on the dock or monitor contact area. Do not scrape contacts aggressively or disassemble the device.

Expected outcome: Contacts are clean, aligned, and unobstructed. If physical damage is found, remove the affected component from service.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good AC Power Cord
steps.instructions:
Swap the AC cord with a verified compatible cord from a working setup.

Expected outcome: If the issue clears, the original power cord is defective and should be replaced.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Power Supply or Dock When Available
steps.instructions:
Test the MX100 with a known-good docking station or approved charger. Also test the suspect dock with a known-good MX100 if this can be done safely and without disrupting patient care.

Expected outcome:
If the issue follows the dock, suspect dock or dock power path failure.
If the issue follows the MX100, suspect monitor-side charging or docking interface issue.
If the issue follows the power supply, replace the power supply.

steps.title: Check Battery Status While Connected to External Power
steps.instructions:
Observe whether the battery icon, charge indicator, or power status changes after connecting to the dock or AC power. Allow a short observation period if the battery is deeply discharged.

Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes external power and begins charging or maintaining battery level.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power Loss
steps.instructions:
With the device safely off patient use, gently move the AC cord, power brick, dock cable, and monitor in the dock while observing the power or charging indicator. Do not force connectors.

Expected outcome: No flicker, dropout, restart, or charging interruption occurs. Any intermittent behavior suggests a loose cable, worn dock contact, damaged adapter, or internal connector issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Dock Is Properly Mounted
steps.instructions:
If the docking station is mounted to a wall rail, roll stand, or transport mount, confirm it is stable and not flexing when the monitor is inserted.

Expected outcome: The dock remains mechanically stable and allows the MX100 to seat correctly.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions:
If the monitor is not in patient use, power it off, disconnect external power, reconnect AC power, reseat it in the dock, and power it back on.

Expected outcome: Temporary software or power-state issues clear. If charging or AC recognition returns, monitor for recurrence before returning to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Failure After Basic Corrections
steps.instructions:
If the issue returns after reseating, cable replacement, outlet verification, and known-good dock or power supply testing, do not continue repeated resets or patient-area testing.

Expected outcome: Common external causes have been ruled out and repair evaluation is needed.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue MX100 was not charging when placed in the docking station.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the MX100 charged normally on a known-good dock, but the original dock had intermittent power when the AC adapter cable was moved.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the defective docking station from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation; MX100 monitor was tested on a known-good dock and returned to service.

helpfulDetails: AC power cord inspected or swapped
Power supply condition and indicator light status
Docking station tested with known-good monitor
Charging icon or battery behavior observed
Any intermittent power loss when cable or dock was moved
Connector damage, debris, bent contacts, or loose mounting found
Unusual heat, smell, or visible damage

title: Philips IntelliVue MX100 Patient Monitor - Wi-Fi / Network Communication Dropout
description: Troubleshooting IntelliVue MX100 Wi-Fi, network dropout, central station visibility, room assignment, and communication interruptions before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips IntelliVue MX100 is not the only active source of patient surveillance before troubleshooting network communication. If the patient requires continuous monitoring, have clinical staff move the patient to another working monitor, alternate central station connection, or appropriate bedside monitoring method first. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained while communication troubleshooting is performed safely.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: MX100 drops off the central station; Waveforms or numerics disappear intermittently; Monitor shows network disconnected or communication loss; Patient assignment does not follow the monitor; Dropout happens only during transport; Dropout occurs in one room, one area, or across multiple areas; Wi-Fi icon or signal indicator changes during the event. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to Wi-Fi coverage, patient assignment, network configuration, monitor behavior, or central station communication.

steps.title: Check Whether the Patient Is Actively Assigned Correctly
steps.instructions: Verify the patient, bed, room, sector, or care area assignment is correct at the monitor and central station. A monitor can appear to have a network problem when it is actually assigned incorrectly or not admitted into the expected monitoring location. Expected outcome: The MX100 appears under the correct patient and location. If assignment correction restores communication, stop.

steps.title: Check the Network Status Indicator on the Monitor
steps.instructions: Look for Wi-Fi, LAN, or communication status indicators on the MX100. Note whether the signal is strong, weak, disconnected, or changing. Expected outcome: Network status gives a clear indication of whether the issue is wireless connectivity, network registration, or central station routing.

steps.title: Verify the Issue Is Not Location-Specific
steps.instructions: Ask whether the dropout happens in one room, hallway, transport route, elevator area, or unit. If safe and approved by clinical workflow, compare behavior in a known good location with good wireless coverage. Expected outcome: If the problem follows the location, suspect Wi-Fi coverage, access point, VLAN, network port, or facility network issue rather than monitor hardware.

steps.title: Check for Recent Movement or Transport Use
steps.instructions: Determine whether the MX100 drops communication only when the patient is moved. Wireless patient monitors may lose central communication if they move outside approved coverage areas or between network zones that are not configured for that monitor group. Expected outcome: Dropout pattern is linked to transport path, coverage boundary, or roaming behavior.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is on the Correct Network Profile or Clinical Area
steps.instructions: Verify the MX100 is configured for the correct care area, network, patient sector, and central station destination according to facility policy. Do not change configuration settings unless authorized and trained to do so. Expected outcome: The monitor is communicating with the correct network environment and central station.

steps.title: Check for Central Station or Unit-Wide Issues
steps.instructions: Ask staff if other monitors in the same unit are also dropping out. Check whether the central station is receiving data from nearby monitors. Expected outcome: If multiple devices are affected, escalate to network, clinical informatics, or Philips support as a system/network issue rather than treating the MX100 as an isolated failure.

steps.title: Check Wired Network or Docking Conditions if Used
steps.instructions: If the MX100 is connected through a dock, bedside connection, or wired network path, inspect the external network cable, wall jack, dock connection, and any visible link indicators. Reseat external cables only when safe and permitted. Expected outcome: A loose cable, failed network drop, bad dock connection, or unplugged patch cord is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Compare With a Known Good Monitor
steps.instructions: If available, place a known good MX100 or approved compatible monitor in the same room or network area using the same workflow. Expected outcome: If the known good monitor also drops, suspect network, configuration, room assignment, or central station issue. If only the suspect MX100 drops, continue evaluating the device.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or External Antenna Area Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the MX100 housing, display, connectors, dock contacts, and any protective case or mounting accessory. Look for cracks, liquid intrusion signs, damaged connectors, or impact damage. Expected outcome: No obvious physical condition is interfering with wireless or dock communication.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the device is not in active patient use, perform a controlled restart according to facility practice. Expected outcome: Temporary communication lockup clears and the monitor reconnects. If communication returns and remains stable, document the restart and monitor status.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, and Network Registration Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the monitor reconnects normally after restart, retains expected configuration, and displays correct basic system information. Expected outcome: Normal registration suggests a transient network or software communication issue. Failure to reconnect suggests configuration, network, or internal communication failure.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to wireless infrastructure, central station configuration, patient monitoring servers, access points, VLANs, room assignments, or monitor software. Expected outcome: Recent environmental or network changes are considered before replacing or repairing the monitor.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Observation Period
steps.instructions: If the monitor reconnects, observe it in a known good area long enough to confirm stable communication. Watch for repeated dropouts, signal changes, or central station loss. Expected outcome: Communication remains stable, or the dropout is reproduced and documented.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Dropout Repeats on the Same Device
steps.instructions: If the MX100 repeatedly drops communication in known good coverage areas while other monitors remain stable, remove it from clinical use. Expected outcome: A potentially unreliable patient monitor is not returned to service without further evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue MX100 intermittently dropped off the central station during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the MX100 lost network communication in multiple known good locations while other monitors remained connected.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MX100 from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented repeated communication dropout, and sent the unit for bench evaluation/network communication repair.

helpfulDetails: Patient room, bed, unit, and central station sector involved
Whether dropout occurred on Wi-Fi, wired connection, dock, or during transport
Central station message or alarm behavior
Whether other monitors in the area were affected
Whether the issue followed the monitor or stayed with the room
Network cable, dock, or wall jack checked
Any recent network, central station, or software changes
Final device status: returned to service, monitored for stability, or removed from service

title: Philips IntelliVue MX100 Patient Monitor - ECG / SpO2 / NIBP Acquisition Failure
description: Troubleshooting ECG, SpO2, and NIBP acquisition failures caused by accessories, patient connection, settings, or monitor input issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips IntelliVue MX100 is not the only active source of patient monitoring before disconnecting ECG leads, SpO2 sensors, NIBP tubing, or accessories. If the patient requires continuous monitoring, have clinical staff move the patient to another verified monitor first. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains safe while the suspect MX100 is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm Which Parameter Is Failing
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the issue affects ECG, SpO2, NIBP, or all three. Determine whether the failure is constant, intermittent, or only occurs during movement, transport, docking, or after patient admission. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one acquisition path, multiple accessories, patient setup, configuration, or monitor hardware.

steps.title: Check for On-Screen Messages or Missing Parameter Tiles
steps.instructions: Look for lead-off messages, SpO2 sensor messages, NIBP timeout messages, dashed values, missing waveforms, or unavailable parameter indicators. Expected outcome: The monitor provides clues about whether the issue is accessory-related, patient-related, setup-related, or internal.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Patient and Monitoring Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is admitted or assigned correctly if the monitor is connected to a central station or networked monitoring system. Check that the expected parameters are enabled and visible on the screen layout. Expected outcome: Parameter failure is not being mistaken for a patient assignment, display layout, or configuration issue.

steps.title: Check ECG Electrodes and Skin Contact
steps.instructions: Inspect ECG electrodes for dryness, poor adhesion, expired pads, loose snaps, hair, lotion, sweat, or poor skin preparation. Replace electrodes if questionable. Expected outcome: ECG waveform and heart rate return if the issue was caused by poor electrode contact. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Lead Set and Trunk Cable
steps.instructions: Check the ECG lead wires and trunk cable for bent pins, cracked insulation, loose snaps, pulled strain reliefs, fluid contamination, or intermittent connection when gently moved. Swap with a known-good ECG cable set when available. Expected outcome: If ECG acquisition returns with a known-good cable, replace the defective accessory and stop.

steps.title: Check SpO2 Sensor Placement and Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the SpO2 sensor is properly positioned, appropriate for the patient, and not placed on a cold, poorly perfused, moving, or restricted extremity. Inspect for damaged sensor clips, worn adhesive, or cable kinks. Expected outcome: SpO2 value and pleth waveform return if the issue was caused by sensor placement or patient signal quality. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Sensor and Interface Cable
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor and cable. Avoid assuming monitor failure until the sensor and cable have been ruled out. Expected outcome: If SpO2 acquisition works with the known-good sensor or cable, remove the defective accessory from service and stop.

steps.title: Check NIBP Cuff Size and Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the cuff is the correct size, applied snugly, aligned properly, and placed on an appropriate limb. Make sure the cuff is not over clothing, folded, loose, or leaking. Expected outcome: NIBP readings complete successfully if the issue was caused by cuff size or placement. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Hose and Cuff for Leaks or Blockage
steps.instructions: Check the cuff bladder, hose, fittings, and connectors for cracks, loose connections, pinching, fluid contamination, or audible leaks during inflation. Swap with a known-good cuff and hose. Expected outcome: If NIBP completes with known-good accessories, replace the failed cuff or hose and stop.

steps.title: Check the MX100 Input Connectors
steps.instructions: Inspect ECG, SpO2, and NIBP connection points for bent pins, damaged ports, loose fit, debris, or signs of fluid intrusion. Do not force connectors into the monitor. Expected outcome: Visible connector damage supports removing the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the MX100 is not connected to an active patient, restart the monitor and allow it to fully boot. Recheck ECG, SpO2, and NIBP using known-good accessories. Expected outcome: A temporary software or acquisition lockup may clear. If acquisition returns, document the restart and monitor performance.

steps.title: Test the Monitor Off Patient Using Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: On the bench, connect known-good ECG leads, SpO2 sensor, and NIBP cuff/hose using approved test methods or simulators available to Clinical Engineering. Expected outcome: If all parameters pass with known-good accessories, the original failure was likely accessory, patient setup, or use-condition related.

steps.title: Look for Pattern Across Multiple Parameters
steps.instructions: If ECG, SpO2, and NIBP all fail at the same time, consider shared causes such as configuration, power instability, software lockup, damaged connector area, fluid exposure, or internal acquisition failure. Expected outcome: Multiple simultaneous parameter failures increase suspicion of monitor-level failure after external causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Check Battery and External Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor has stable power and is not shutting down, rebooting, or dropping parameters during low battery or charging transitions. Expected outcome: Parameter acquisition should remain stable on known-good AC power or a properly charged battery.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Failure Remains
steps.instructions: If ECG, SpO2, or NIBP acquisition still fails after known-good accessories, safe restart, proper setup, and bench checks, remove the MX100 from clinical use. Expected outcome: The device is properly escalated instead of repeatedly returned with an unresolved monitoring issue.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue MX100 was not acquiring ECG, SpO2, or NIBP readings during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found that ECG leads, SpO2 sensor, and NIBP cuff/hose were checked with known-good accessories, but parameter acquisition remained intermittent.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MX100 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation due to suspected monitor acquisition or connector failure.

helpfulDetails: Whether ECG, SpO2, NIBP, or all parameters failed
Exact alarm, INOP, or message displayed
Whether waveforms, numerics, or both were missing
Patient assignment or central station behavior
SpO2 sensor and interface cable swapped
Whether NIBP inflated, timed out, or failed to start
Any connector damage, looseness, fluid exposure, heat, smell, or unusual behavior

title: Philips IntelliVue MX100 Patient Monitor - Patient Assignment / Central Station Communication Issue
description: Troubleshooting MX100 patient assignment, central station visibility, network connection, sector assignment, and communication issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips IntelliVue MX100 is not the only active source of patient surveillance before troubleshooting communication or assignment issues. If continuous monitoring is required, have clinical staff verify local bedside monitoring, central station visibility, or move the patient to another working monitor first. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains active and safe while the communication issue is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they are seeing. Determine whether the issue is: Patient not visible at the central station; Patient assigned to the wrong bed, room, or sector; Monitor visible but no waveforms or numerics are transmitting; Central station shows communication loss, offline, or no monitor; Admission, discharge, or transfer information is incorrect; Issue occurs after transport, docking, undocking, or room transfer; One MX100 is affected or multiple monitors are affected. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient assignment, monitor setup, network connection, central station configuration, or possible device communication failure.

steps.title: Verify Local Monitoring First
steps.instructions: Check whether the MX100 itself is displaying patient parameters correctly at the bedside. Confirm ECG, SpO2, NIBP, or other connected parameters are present locally. Expected outcome: If local monitoring is working but central station communication is not, the issue is more likely assignment, network, or central station related.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Patient Assignment on the MX100
steps.instructions: Review the patient name, MRN, bed label, room number, or assigned monitoring location on the MX100, depending on site configuration. Correct any obvious assignment mismatch only if permitted by facility workflow. Expected outcome: The monitor is assigned to the correct patient and location before deeper troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Recent Patient Movement or Transfer
steps.instructions: Ask whether the patient was recently admitted, discharged, transferred, moved rooms, transported, or reconnected to monitoring. Patient assignment problems often occur when the monitor still reflects the previous location or has not been reassigned properly. Expected outcome: Recent workflow changes are identified as a possible cause.

steps.title: Verify the Central Station Bed or Sector Assignment
steps.instructions: At the central station, confirm the correct bed, sector, or care area is selected for the MX100. Look for duplicate beds, unassigned beds, inactive tiles, or a patient assigned to the wrong room. Expected outcome: The central station is looking for the correct device and patient location.

steps.title: Check Wireless or Network Connection Status
steps.instructions: Review the MX100 communication or network status indicators. Look for signs of weak wireless signal, no network connection, LAN disconnect, or communication loss. Expected outcome: The monitor shows an active network connection. If not, continue checking external network causes.

steps.title: Move the Monitor Within the Care Area if Wireless Is Suspected
steps.instructions: If the MX100 communicates wirelessly and the issue appears location-related, move the monitor closer to a known good coverage area if clinically safe and approved. Expected outcome: If communication restores, document a possible wireless coverage or access point issue rather than a monitor failure.

steps.title: Check Physical Docking or Network Accessories
steps.instructions: If the MX100 is connected through a dock, mount, network adapter, or wired network point, confirm it is fully seated and all external cables are secure. Inspect for bent pins, loose connectors, damaged network cables, or unstable docking. Expected outcome: External communication paths are secure and visibly undamaged.

steps.title: Compare Against a Known Working Monitor or Bedside Location
steps.instructions: If available, compare the affected MX100 with another known working MX100 in the same area. Determine whether the issue follows the monitor, the room, the dock, the network jack, or the central station assignment. Expected outcome: The fault is isolated to the device, location, network path, or assignment workflow.

steps.title: Check for Duplicate or Conflicting Patient Assignments
steps.instructions: Look for another monitor or central station tile already assigned to the same patient, bed, or room. Duplicate assignments can prevent proper display or cause staff to see the patient in the wrong location. Expected outcome: Only one correct active assignment exists for the patient and bed.

steps.title: Review Alarm and Message Behavior
steps.instructions: Check both the MX100 and central station for communication-related messages, offline indicators, missing waveform tiles, or assignment prompts. Expected outcome: The displayed messages help separate a patient assignment issue from a network communication issue.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the patient is not actively dependent on the monitor, restart the MX100 according to normal facility practice. Do not power cycle the device during active monitoring unless staff have moved the patient to another verified monitoring method. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication lockups may clear. If the issue resolves, verify central station visibility before returning to service.

steps.title: Verify Communication After Correction
steps.instructions: Confirm the central station displays the correct patient, room, bed, waveforms, numerics, and alarm status. Ask clinical staff to confirm the patient appears in the expected monitoring sector. Expected outcome: The MX100 is communicating properly and the patient assignment is accurate.

steps.title: Stop if the Issue Is Resolved
steps.instructions: If correcting assignment, reseating the monitor, restoring network connection, or correcting the central station bed location resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the final working condition. Expected outcome: The device remains in service only after communication and patient assignment are verified.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips IntelliVue MX100 was monitoring locally but the patient was not appearing correctly at the central station.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the MX100 assigned to the wrong bed location after a patient room transfer.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected the patient/bed assignment, verified live waveforms and numerics at the central station, and returned the MX100 to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or communication loss messages
Accessories, docks, cables, network adapters, or bedside locations swapped
Power behavior, restart behavior, or whether a power cycle was performed
Environmental factors such as wireless coverage area, room transfer, docking, undocking, or transport history
Indicator lights, wireless indicators, LAN status, offline messages, missing waveform tiles, or assignment prompts
Central station bed or sector checked
Patient name, room, or bed assignment verified
Whether the issue followed the monitor or stayed with the room

title: Philips SureSigns VM Series Patient Monitor - ECG Waveform Artifact / Lead-Off Issue
description: Troubleshooting ECG artifact, noisy waveform, intermittent tracing, lead-off messages, cable problems, and electrode connection issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips SureSigns VM monitor is not the only active source of cardiac monitoring before disconnecting ECG leads, removing electrodes, or swapping cables. If continuous monitoring is required, have clinical staff move the patient to another verified monitor first. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained safely while the ECG issue is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact ECG Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: ECG artifact; Wandering baseline; Lead-off message; Intermittent ECG dropout; Flatline or absent waveform; One lead missing; All ECG leads missing; Heart rate reading incorrectly; Artifact only during movement; Issue follows the cable, patient, or monitor. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient setup, electrodes, lead wires, ECG cable, settings, or monitor hardware.

steps.title: Check the Patient Connection First When Safe
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff verify that ECG electrodes are properly attached to clean, dry skin and are placed in the correct locations. Loose electrodes, poor skin prep, sweat, lotion, hair, or dried electrode gel can cause artifact or lead-off messages. Expected outcome: If the waveform stabilizes or the lead-off message clears, the issue was patient connection related. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Replace Questionable ECG Electrodes
steps.instructions: Ask staff to replace the electrodes with fresh, non-expired electrodes from a known-good package. Electrodes can dry out or lose adhesion, even if they appear usable. Expected outcome: A stable ECG waveform returns and the lead-off message clears. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Lead Wire Attachment at Each Electrode
steps.instructions: Confirm each snap or clip connection is secure and not loose, corroded, bent, or contaminated. Gently move the wires near the electrode connections while observing the waveform. Expected outcome: No artifact should appear when the lead wires are lightly moved. If movement causes artifact, replace the lead set.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Lead Set
steps.instructions: Check the patient lead wires for cuts, stretching, exposed conductors, damaged snaps, bent clips, missing labels, or strain relief damage. Pay close attention to areas near the patient end and connector end. Expected outcome: The ECG lead set is physically intact. If damaged, replace it and retest.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good ECG Lead Set
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible ECG lead set and repeat ECG monitoring on a simulator or appropriate test setup. Expected outcome: If the artifact or lead-off issue clears, the original lead set was defective. Replace the accessory and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Trunk Cable or Main ECG Cable, If Used
steps.instructions: If the SureSigns VM configuration uses a separate ECG trunk cable, inspect it for bent pins, cracked housing, loose strain relief, liquid contamination, or intermittent connection. Expected outcome: Cable and connector are clean, secure, and undamaged. If damage is found, replace the cable and retest.

steps.title: Verify the ECG Cable Is Fully Seated at the Monitor
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the ECG cable at the monitor, ensuring it is fully inserted and secure. Do not force the connector. Check for obvious bent pins, debris, or cracked connector housing. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the ECG connection and displays a stable waveform.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect Lead Selection or Display Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed ECG lead is appropriate and that the monitor is not set to a lead that is unavailable, disconnected, or producing poor signal quality. Change to another available lead to see whether the issue is isolated to one lead. Expected outcome: A usable ECG waveform is displayed when a valid lead is selected.

steps.title: Check ECG Gain, Filter, and Monitoring Mode Settings
steps.instructions: Review ECG size/gain, filter mode, and monitoring configuration. Incorrect settings can make artifact appear worse or make the waveform difficult to interpret. Expected outcome: ECG waveform appears appropriately scaled and stable after settings are corrected.

steps.title: Rule Out Patient Movement or External Interference
steps.instructions: Ask whether artifact occurs during patient movement, shivering, transport, bed movement, procedure activity, or contact with other equipment. Also check for nearby sources of electrical interference when practical. Expected outcome: If artifact occurs only during movement or interference, the monitor may be functioning normally and the cause should be documented.

steps.title: Test with an ECG Simulator
steps.instructions: Remove the monitor from patient use and connect a compatible ECG simulator using known-good ECG cables. Verify whether the SureSigns VM displays a clean waveform and stable heart rate. Expected outcome: A clean simulator waveform indicates the monitor ECG input is likely functional and the issue is probably patient setup, electrodes, or accessories.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor if Needed
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves inaccurate heart rate or persistent artifact, compare the same simulator signal or known-good lead set on another verified monitor. Expected outcome: If the problem follows the cable or setup, replace the accessory. If it stays with the SureSigns VM, escalate the monitor for repair.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination at the ECG Input
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor ECG connector area for cracked plastic, loose connection, bent pins, fluid residue, corrosion, or signs of impact damage. Expected outcome: Connector should be clean, stable, and undamaged. If damage or contamination is present, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the ECG issue appears related to a monitor freeze, configuration hang, or intermittent recognition, power cycle the monitor after confirming it is not actively needed for patient monitoring. Expected outcome: ECG function returns normally after restart. If the issue returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: Confirm Final Function
steps.instructions: After replacing electrodes, cables, or correcting setup, verify ECG waveform quality, heart rate display, lead selection, and absence of lead-off messages. Expected outcome: The SureSigns VM displays a stable ECG waveform and reliable heart rate.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported ECG artifact and intermittent lead-off messages on a Philips SureSigns VM Series patient monitor.
CCR.Cause: Found worn ECG lead wires with intermittent connection near the patient snap leads; monitor displayed a stable waveform with a known-good lead set.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the ECG lead set, verified clean ECG waveform and stable heart rate using simulator testing, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred on one patient or multiple patients
Which ECG lead showed artifact or lead-off
Whether the lead set was swapped
Whether a trunk cable was used and inspected
Any visible damage, fluid residue, or corrosion

title: Philips SureSigns VM Series Patient Monitor - NIBP Measurement Timeout
description: Troubleshooting SureSigns VM Series NIBP timeout issues caused by cuff, hose, leak, patient movement, setup, or monitor problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the SureSigns VM monitor is not delaying patient care or being repeatedly cycled on a patient while NIBP readings are failing. If blood pressure is clinically needed, have staff use another working monitor or a manual blood pressure method first. Expected outcome: Patient blood pressure monitoring continues safely while the suspect monitor is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact NIBP Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what happened during the measurement attempt. Determine whether the cuff does not inflate, inflates but times out, inflates slowly, deflates before completion, repeatedly retries, fails only on one patient, or fails on multiple patients. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient condition, cuff setup, tubing, leak, settings, or internal NIBP hardware.

steps.title: Check for Patient-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the patient was moving, shivering, talking, restless, or had poor perfusion during the measurement. Also confirm the cuff was placed on an appropriate limb. Expected outcome: If the timeout was caused by movement or poor measurement conditions, reposition the cuff, allow the patient to remain still, repeat the reading once, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Verify Correct Cuff Size and Placement
steps.instructions: Check that the cuff size matches the patient’s arm circumference and that the artery marker is properly aligned. Confirm the cuff is snug but not overly tight. Expected outcome: Incorrect cuff size or placement is corrected. If the monitor completes a normal NIBP cycle afterward, return the device to service.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Cuff for Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the cuff for tears, worn hook-and-loop material, damaged bladder areas, cracked fittings, or signs of air leakage. Expected outcome: A damaged cuff is replaced. If the measurement completes successfully with a known-good cuff, document the accessory failure and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Hose and Connectors
steps.instructions: Check the hose from the cuff to the SureSigns VM monitor. Look for kinks, loose fittings, cracks, pinched tubing, or partially connected fittings. Expected outcome: The hose is fully connected and unobstructed. If correcting the hose resolves the timeout, return the monitor to service.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Cuff and Hose
steps.instructions: Use a compatible known-good NIBP cuff and hose set. Run a test measurement using an approved test setup or appropriate clinical workflow when safe. Expected outcome: If the timeout disappears with known-good accessories, the original cuff or hose was the likely cause.

steps.title: Check the Monitor NIBP Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the NIBP connector on the SureSigns VM monitor for broken plastic, bent fittings, contamination, looseness, or difficulty securing the hose. Do not force the connector. Expected outcome: If the port is visibly damaged or cannot hold the hose securely, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Listen for Pump Operation During a Measurement Attempt
steps.instructions: Start an NIBP cycle with the proper cuff and hose attached. Listen for pump activity and observe whether the cuff inflates. Expected outcome: If the pump runs and pressure builds, continue checking for leaks or patient/setup issues. If the pump does not run, runs weakly, or sounds abnormal, internal service is likely needed.

steps.title: Check for Air Leaks During Inflation
steps.instructions: During inflation, listen for hissing near the cuff, hose fittings, and monitor connector. Watch whether the cuff inflates then loses pressure quickly. Expected outcome: Any leaking cuff or hose is replaced. If leakage appears to come from inside the monitor or the NIBP port, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Confirm NIBP Settings Are Appropriate
steps.instructions: Verify that the selected patient type, cuff size, and NIBP mode are appropriate for the patient. Check whether repeated automatic cycles are contributing to alarms or failed readings. Expected outcome: Incorrect settings are corrected. If the NIBP measurement completes normally, document the settings correction and stop.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not actively needed for patient monitoring, power cycle the SureSigns VM monitor and repeat the NIBP test with known-good accessories. Expected outcome: If the issue clears after restart and NIBP passes functional testing, monitor for recurrence before returning to service.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: If available, test the same known-good cuff and hose on another SureSigns VM or equivalent monitor. Expected outcome: If the accessories work on another monitor but not on the suspect unit, the issue is likely within the suspect monitor’s NIBP system.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Run a controlled NIBP check according to your facility’s Clinical Engineering process using appropriate test equipment or approved functional verification method. Expected outcome: If the monitor completes a normal inflation, hold, and deflation cycle without timeout, document the result. If it fails, escalate for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips SureSigns VM monitor repeatedly timed out during NIBP measurements and would not produce a blood pressure reading.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the NIBP cuff hose was loose at the monitor connector, preventing stable pressure buildup during inflation.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the NIBP hose, tested with a known-good cuff, completed multiple successful NIBP cycles, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred on one patient or multiple patients
Whether cuff inflated, partially inflated, or did not inflate
Any NIBP timeout, leak, or overpressure message displayed
Whether a known-good cuff and hose were tested
Whether the NIBP port appeared damaged
Whether the outlet or power source was checked if power concerns were also present
Final device status: returned to service, accessory replaced, or removed from service

title: Philips SureSigns VM Series Patient Monitor - SpO2 Sensor or Cable Failure
description: Troubleshooting SureSigns VM Series SpO2 sensor, cable, connector, signal dropout, or sensor recognition failure before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the SureSigns VM monitor is not the only active source of oxygen saturation monitoring before disconnecting sensors or cables. If SpO2 monitoring is clinically required, have staff move the patient to another working monitor or use an approved alternate monitoring method first. Expected outcome: Patient oxygenation monitoring is maintained safely during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact SpO2 Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: No SpO2 reading; Dashed or blank SpO2 value; Sensor not detected; Intermittent SpO2 dropout; Poor pleth waveform; Inaccurate or unstable reading; Alarm or message related to SpO2 sensor, cable, or signal quality. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient condition, sensor placement, cable failure, accessory compatibility, connector issue, or monitor hardware.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Sensor Site When Safe
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor is placed correctly and the site has adequate perfusion. Check for cold extremities, excessive motion, nail polish, artificial nails, poor circulation, bright ambient light, or improper sensor size. Expected outcome: Patient-related signal problems are identified before replacing equipment.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Check the sensor for cracked housing, damaged emitter/detector area, exposed wiring, loose strain relief, contamination, fluid intrusion, or worn adhesive if using a disposable sensor. Expected outcome: A visibly damaged or contaminated sensor is removed from use and replaced.

steps.title: Verify Proper Sensor Application
steps.instructions: Reapply the sensor according to normal clinical use, ensuring the emitter and detector are aligned and the sensor is not too loose or too tight. Expected outcome: A stable SpO2 value and pleth waveform return. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Extension Cable, If Used
steps.instructions: Inspect the extension cable for bent pins, cracked connectors, intermittent strain relief, cuts, kinks, or loose locking connection. Reseat both ends firmly. Expected outcome: A loose or damaged cable connection is corrected. If SpO2 returns, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Sensor With a Known-Good Sensor
steps.instructions: Connect a compatible known-good Philips-approved SpO2 sensor to the same monitor and test on a simulator or appropriate test subject when safe. Expected outcome: If the known-good sensor works, the original sensor is defective or incompatible and should be replaced.

steps.title: Swap the Extension Cable With a Known-Good Cable
steps.instructions: If the problem remains after replacing the sensor, test with a known-good compatible SpO2 extension cable. Expected outcome: If the issue clears, replace the failed cable and return the monitor to service after verification.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor SpO2 Connector
steps.instructions: Look at the SpO2 input port for bent pins, debris, cracked plastic, looseness, liquid residue, or signs of physical damage. Do not force a connector into the port. Expected outcome: Connector damage or contamination is identified. If the port is damaged, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Check Whether the Failure Follows the Accessory or the Monitor
steps.instructions: Test the suspect sensor and cable on another compatible SureSigns VM monitor, if available. Then test known-good accessories on the suspect monitor. Expected outcome: The failure is isolated to the sensor, cable, or monitor SpO2 input path.

steps.title: Review SpO2 Settings and Display Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm SpO2 is enabled and being displayed as an active parameter. Check that the parameter has not been hidden, disabled, or affected by an incorrect profile or configuration. Expected outcome: A configuration issue is corrected without replacing hardware.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Dropout During Movement
steps.instructions: Gently move the cable near the sensor end, extension connection, and monitor connector while observing the SpO2 reading and pleth waveform. Do not perform this on an active patient. Expected outcome: Intermittent dropout during cable movement suggests cable or connector failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not actively supporting patient care, power cycle the device and reconnect a known-good SpO2 sensor and cable. Expected outcome: Temporary software or recognition issues clear. If resolved, verify stable operation before return to service.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check
steps.instructions: Use a compatible SpO2 simulator or approved test method to confirm the monitor detects the sensor, displays SpO2, and maintains a stable waveform or numeric reading. Expected outcome: The SureSigns VM passes a basic SpO2 functional check with known-good accessories.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips SureSigns VM monitor would not display an SpO2 reading and showed intermittent sensor recognition.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the SpO2 extension cable had an intermittent connection when flexed near the monitor-side connector.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the SpO2 extension cable, verified stable SpO2 reading with a known-good sensor, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient was moved to alternate monitoring before troubleshooting
Exact SpO2 message or alarm observed
SpO2 value blank, dashed, unstable, or intermittent
Pleth waveform present, poor, or absent
Whether issue followed the sensor, cable, or monitor
Environmental factors such as motion, cold extremities, bright ambient light, nail polish, artificial nails, or poor circulation
Indicator lights, sensor recognition behavior, or displayed SpO2 status

title: Philips SureSigns VM Series Patient Monitor - Recorder / Printer Failure
description: Troubleshooting SureSigns VM recorder paper feed, print quality, door, paper, and printer response issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips SureSigns VM monitor is not the only active monitor being relied on for patient care before removing paper, opening the recorder door, or restarting the monitor. If monitoring is required, have staff use another verified monitor or continue observation while troubleshooting is performed. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained while the recorder issue is evaluated safely.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Recorder Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what failed. Determine whether the issue is: Recorder does not print; Paper does not feed; Paper jam; Recorder door will not close; Blank printout; Faded or unreadable printout; Intermittent printing; Error message related to recorder, paper, or printer; Monitor works normally except for printing. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to paper, door, print command, recorder mechanism, or internal printer failure.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor Is Powered On and Functioning Normally
steps.instructions: Confirm the SureSigns VM powers on, displays parameters, and responds to normal user input. If the entire monitor is frozen, blank, or unresponsive, troubleshoot the monitor power or display issue first. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed to be recorder-specific, not a full monitor failure.

steps.title: Check for Recorder or Paper Messages
steps.instructions: Look for any message indicating printer error, paper out, recorder door open, paper jam, or recorder unavailable. Clear only external conditions that can be safely corrected. Expected outcome: On-screen messages guide whether the issue is paper, door, jam, or recorder hardware related.

steps.title: Inspect the Recorder Door
steps.instructions: Open and close the recorder door fully. Confirm it latches securely and is not cracked, loose, or blocked by paper. A door that is not fully closed may prevent printing even if paper is loaded. Expected outcome: Door closes normally and any door-open condition clears.

steps.title: Check Paper Roll Installation
steps.instructions: Verify the correct recorder paper is installed and that the roll is seated properly. Confirm the paper feeds from the correct direction for thermal printing. If the paper is backwards, the recorder may feed paper but produce a blank strip. Expected outcome: Paper is loaded correctly and positioned for printing.

steps.title: Replace the Paper Roll
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible thermal paper roll. Do not assume the existing roll is usable based only on appearance. Old, incorrect, or non-thermal paper can cause blank or poor-quality printouts. Expected outcome: Recorder prints normally with verified paper. If resolved, return the device to service.

steps.title: Check for Paper Jam or Torn Paper
steps.instructions: Inspect the paper path for torn paper, folded edges, stuck labels, debris, or pieces of paper caught near the feed path. Remove only loose, visible obstructions without forcing the mechanism or disassembling the recorder. Expected outcome: Paper path is clear and paper feeds smoothly.

steps.title: Test Manual Paper Feed
steps.instructions: Use the recorder feed function, if available, to confirm paper advances. If the motor runs but paper does not move, suspect paper loading, roller contact, door latch, or feed mechanism failure. If there is no sound or movement, suspect recorder power/control failure. Expected outcome: Paper advances consistently during feed testing.

steps.title: Test a Print Function
steps.instructions: Initiate a simple printout such as a strip recording, alarm recording, trend print, or available recorder test function depending on unit configuration. Expected outcome: Recorder starts, feeds paper, and produces readable output.

steps.title: Inspect Print Quality
steps.instructions: Review the printout for fading, missing sections, uneven darkness, blank output, or intermittent lines. Confirm the issue is not caused by reversed paper or poor paper quality before suspecting the thermal printhead. Expected outcome: Print is readable and consistent across the strip.

steps.title: Check Recorder Configuration or Print Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm printing has not been disabled, redirected, or limited by configuration. Verify staff are using the correct print command and not expecting a function that is unavailable on that unit. Expected outcome: Recorder settings and user command match the expected print behavior.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not actively monitoring a patient, power cycle the SureSigns VM and retest the recorder. A temporary software lockup can prevent recorder response even when the monitor otherwise appears normal. Expected outcome: Recorder function returns after restart. If resolved, document and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Unit if Available
steps.instructions: Use the same type of paper and same print command on another SureSigns VM monitor. If the paper and workflow work on another monitor, the issue likely remains with the suspect unit. Expected outcome: External paper and user process are ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the recorder door, hinge, latch, paper tray, and visible feed area for cracks, fluid residue, impact damage, melted plastic, unusual smell, heat, or binding. Do not continue using a device with signs of fluid intrusion, overheating, or mechanical damage. Expected outcome: Device is confirmed safe for continued evaluation or removed from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Philips SureSigns VM monitor would not print patient strips from the recorder.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the recorder door latched properly and a known-good paper roll was installed, but the recorder would not feed or print during test operation.
CCR.Resolution: Monitor was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation for suspected recorder assembly failure.

helpfulDetails: Whether the monitor otherwise powered on normally
Exact recorder message or error displayed
Whether paper was missing, incorrect, reversed, or jammed
Whether a known-good thermal paper roll was installed
Whether the recorder door latched securely
Whether the recorder motor made noise
Whether output was blank, faded, or incomplete
Whether the issue occurred during strip print, trend print, or alarm recording
Any signs of physical damage, fluid residue, unusual heat, or smell

title: Philips SureSigns VM Series Patient Monitor - Power Supply / Battery Issue
description: Troubleshooting SureSigns VM power, charging, battery runtime, AC adapter, power cord, and startup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips SureSigns VM monitor is not the only active source of patient monitoring before removing power, disconnecting the battery, or rebooting the device. If the patient requires active monitoring, have clinical staff move the patient to another working monitor first. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the suspect monitor is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Power Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue is: Monitor will not power on; Monitor powers on only with AC power; Monitor shuts off when unplugged; Battery will not charge; Battery runtime is short; Battery icon does not show charging; Power supply light is off or flickering; Unit turns off intermittently; Device was dropped, exposed to liquid, or has a damaged power connector. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to AC power, external power supply, battery condition, charging circuit, or physical damage.

steps.title: Check the Wall Outlet First
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor is plugged into a known working hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with another approved device or outlet tester if appropriate. Expected outcome: A failed outlet or switched outlet is ruled out before suspecting the monitor.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and AC Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the power cord, brick-style power supply, and connector for cuts, bent pins, loose fit, exposed wiring, strain damage, heat discoloration, or missing ground pin if applicable. Expected outcome: Damaged or unsafe external power components are identified and removed from use.

steps.title: Verify the Power Supply Indicator Light
steps.instructions: If the AC adapter has an indicator light, confirm it illuminates steadily when connected to the outlet and monitor. Expected outcome: A steady indicator suggests the adapter is receiving AC power. No light or flickering may indicate outlet, cord, or adapter failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Power Connector Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions: Make sure the DC power connector is fully inserted into the SureSigns VM monitor and is not loose, angled, or easily interrupted when gently moved. Expected outcome: The monitor receives stable external power without intermittent shutdowns.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Power Supply if Available
steps.instructions: Use a compatible, approved Philips power supply from a working SureSigns VM monitor if available. Do not substitute an unapproved adapter. Expected outcome: If the monitor works normally with a known good adapter, the original power supply or cord is likely defective. Replace the external power supply and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Observe the Monitor During Startup
steps.instructions: Connect the monitor to verified AC power and attempt to power it on. Watch for display activity, indicator lights, fan or speaker activity if present, battery icon behavior, startup tones, or error messages. Expected outcome: Startup behavior helps separate a no-power condition from a battery-only or charging-related complaint.

steps.title: Check Battery Status on the Display
steps.instructions: Once powered on, review the battery icon or battery status indicator. Look for low battery, charging symbol, battery missing, battery fault, or abnormal charge percentage behavior. Expected outcome: The displayed battery condition helps identify whether the monitor recognizes the battery and charger.

steps.title: Allow the Battery to Charge on Verified AC Power
steps.instructions: If the monitor powers on with AC but has low battery, leave it connected to known good AC power long enough to confirm charging behavior. Expected outcome: Battery percentage should increase or the charging indicator should remain active. If it does, return the device only after adequate charge and functional verification.

steps.title: Check Whether the Monitor Runs on Battery Power
steps.instructions: After confirming the battery has had time to charge, disconnect AC power only when the device is not being used on a patient. Observe whether the monitor remains powered. Expected outcome: If the monitor immediately shuts off when unplugged, suspect a failed battery, unrecognized battery, or internal charging/power issue.

steps.title: Compare Runtime to Expected Use
steps.instructions: If staff report short runtime, confirm whether the battery was fully charged before use and whether the monitor has heavy parameter use, frequent alarms, or an aging battery. Expected outcome: A repeatable short runtime after full charging supports battery replacement or further bench evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Area Externally
steps.instructions: If the battery is externally accessible, inspect only to the level allowed by normal Clinical Engineering handling.
